**Manhattan**[[b]](#cite_note-9) is the most densely populated and geographically smallest of the [five boroughs](/wiki/Five_boroughs "Five boroughs") of [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"). Coextensive with **New York County**, Manhattan is the [smallest county by area](/wiki/County_statistics_of_the_United_States#Smallest,_largest,_and_average_area_per_state_and_territory "County statistics of the United States") in the U.S. state of [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"), and one of the [smallest in the United States](/wiki/List_of_United_States_counties_and_county_equivalents "List of United States counties and county equivalents"). Located almost entirely on Manhattan Island near the southern tip of the state, Manhattan is centrally located in the [Northeast megalopolis](/wiki/Northeast_megalopolis "Northeast megalopolis") and represents the urban core of the [New York metropolitan area](/wiki/New_York_metropolitan_area "New York metropolitan area").[[8]](#cite_note-10) Manhattan serves as New York City's [economic](/wiki/Economy_of_New_York_City "Economy of New York City") and [administrative](/wiki/Government_of_New_York_City "Government of New York City") center and has been described as the cultural, financial, [media](/wiki/Media_in_New_York_City "Media in New York City"), and [entertainment](/wiki/Show_business "Show business") capital of the world.[[9]](#cite_note-11)[[10]](#cite_note-12)[[11]](#cite_note-13)[[12]](#cite_note-ManhattanMediaEntertainmentCapital-14)

Before European colonization, present-day Manhattan was part of [Lenape](/wiki/Lenape "Lenape") territory.[[13]](#cite_note-:1-15) European settlement began with the establishment of a [trading post](/wiki/Trading_post "Trading post") by [Dutch colonists](/wiki/Dutch_colonization_of_the_Americas "Dutch colonization of the Americas") in 1624 on Manhattan Island; the post was named [New Amsterdam](/wiki/New_Amsterdam "New Amsterdam") in 1626. The territory came under English control in 1664 and was renamed New York after King [Charles II of England](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England") granted the lands to his brother, the [Duke of York](/wiki/James_II_of_England "James II of England").[[14]](#cite_note-16) New York, based in present-day [Lower Manhattan](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan "Lower Manhattan"), served as the [capital of the United States](/wiki/List_of_capitals_in_the_United_States#Capitals_of_the_US "List of capitals in the United States") from 1785 until 1790.[[15]](#cite_note-senate-17) The [Statue of Liberty](/wiki/Statue_of_Liberty "Statue of Liberty") in [New York Harbor](/wiki/New_York_Harbor "New York Harbor") greeted millions of arriving immigrants [in the late 19th century](/wiki/History_of_immigration_to_the_United_States#1850_to_1930 "History of immigration to the United States") and is a world symbol of the United States and its ideals.[[16]](#cite_note-18) Manhattan became a borough during the [consolidation of New York City in 1898](/wiki/Consolidation_of_New_York_City "Consolidation of New York City"), and houses [New York City Hall](/wiki/New_York_City_Hall "New York City Hall"), the seat of the [city's government](/wiki/Government_of_New_York_City "Government of New York City").[[17]](#cite_note-19) [Harlem](/wiki/Harlem "Harlem") in [Upper Manhattan](/wiki/Upper_Manhattan "Upper Manhattan") became the center of what is now known as the cultural [Harlem Renaissance](/wiki/Harlem_Renaissance "Harlem Renaissance") in the 1920s. The [Stonewall Inn](/wiki/Stonewall_Inn "Stonewall Inn") in [Greenwich Village](/wiki/Greenwich_Village "Greenwich Village"), part of the [Stonewall National Monument](/wiki/Stonewall_National_Monument "Stonewall National Monument"), is considered the [birthplace in 1969](/wiki/Stonewall_riots "Stonewall riots") of the modern [gay-rights movement](/wiki/Gay-rights_movement "Gay-rights movement"), cementing Manhattan's central role in [LGBTQ culture](/wiki/LGBTQ_culture_in_New_York_City#Manhattan "LGBTQ culture in New York City").[[18]](#cite_note-NPS99000562-20)[[19]](#cite_note-ObamaStonewall-21) Lower Manhattan was the site of the original [World Trade Center](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_(1973%E2%80%932001) "World Trade Center (1973–2001)"), which was [destroyed](/wiki/Collapse_of_the_World_Trade_Center "Collapse of the World Trade Center") during the [September 11 terrorist attacks](/wiki/September_11_terrorist_attacks "September 11 terrorist attacks") in 2001.

Situated on [one of the world's largest natural harbors](/wiki/New_York_Harbor "New York Harbor"), the borough is bounded by the [Hudson](/wiki/Hudson_River "Hudson River"), [East](/wiki/East_River "East River"), and [Harlem](/wiki/Harlem_River "Harlem River") rivers and includes [several small adjacent islands](/wiki/List_of_smaller_islands_in_New_York_City "List of smaller islands in New York City"), including [Roosevelt](/wiki/Roosevelt_Island "Roosevelt Island"), [U Thant](/wiki/U_Thant_Island "U Thant Island"), and [Randalls and Wards Islands](/wiki/Randalls_and_Wards_Islands "Randalls and Wards Islands"). It also includes the small neighborhood of [Marble Hill](/wiki/Marble_Hill,_Manhattan "Marble Hill, Manhattan"), now on the [U.S. mainland](/wiki/U.S._mainland "U.S. mainland"). Manhattan Island is divided into three informally bounded components, each cutting across the borough's long axis: Lower Manhattan, [Midtown](/wiki/Midtown_Manhattan "Midtown Manhattan"), and Upper Manhattan. Manhattan is one of the most densely populated locations in the world, with a [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census") population of 1,694,250 living in a land area of 22.66 square miles (58.69 km2),[[5]](#cite_note-ManhattanQuickFacts-5)[[20]](#cite_note-22) or 72,918 residents per square mile (28,154 residents/km2), and its residential property has the highest sale price per square foot in the United States.[[21]](#cite_note-ManhattanPricePerSquareFoot-23)

Manhattan produces the [highest U.S. county GDP by both absolute and per capita measures](/wiki/List_of_U.S._counties_by_GDP "List of U.S. counties by GDP"). The borough hosts [Billionaires' Row](/wiki/Billionaires%27_Row "Billionaires' Row") and [Wall Street](/wiki/Wall_Street "Wall Street"), as well as the world's two [largest stock exchanges](/wiki/Largest_stock_exchanges "Largest stock exchanges") by total [market capitalization](/wiki/Market_capitalization "Market capitalization"), the [New York Stock Exchange](/wiki/New_York_Stock_Exchange "New York Stock Exchange") and [Nasdaq](/wiki/Nasdaq "Nasdaq").[[22]](#cite_note-LargestExchanges-24) Many multinational [media conglomerates](/wiki/Media_conglomerate "Media conglomerate") are based in Manhattan, as are numerous colleges and universities, such as [Columbia University](/wiki/Columbia_University "Columbia University"), [New York University](/wiki/New_York_University "New York University"), [Rockefeller University](/wiki/Rockefeller_University "Rockefeller University"), and the [City University of New York](/wiki/City_University_of_New_York "City University of New York"). The [headquarters of the United Nations](/wiki/Headquarters_of_the_United_Nations "Headquarters of the United Nations") is located in the [Turtle Bay](/wiki/Turtle_Bay,_Manhattan "Turtle Bay, Manhattan") neighborhood of Midtown Manhattan. Manhattan hosts three of the world's top 10 most-visited tourist attractions: [Times Square](/wiki/Times_Square "Times Square"), [Central Park](/wiki/Central_Park "Central Park"), and [Grand Central Terminal](/wiki/Grand_Central_Terminal "Grand Central Terminal").[[23]](#cite_note-Ann_Shields-25) [New York Penn Station](/wiki/New_York_Penn_Station "New York Penn Station") is the busiest transportation hub in the [Western Hemisphere](/wiki/Western_Hemisphere "Western Hemisphere").[[24]](#cite_note-26) [Chinatown](/wiki/Chinatown,_Manhattan "Chinatown, Manhattan") has the highest concentration of [Chinese people](/wiki/Chinese_people_in_New_York_City "Chinese people in New York City") in the Western Hemisphere.[[25]](#cite_note-27) [Fifth Avenue](/wiki/Fifth_Avenue "Fifth Avenue") has been ranked as the most expensive shopping street in the world, before falling to second in 2024.[[26]](#cite_note-28)[[27]](#cite_note-FifthAvenueMostExpensiveStreetOnEarth-29) The borough hosts many prominent [bridges](/wiki/Bridges_and_tunnels_in_New_York_City "Bridges and tunnels in New York City"), [tunnels](/wiki/Bridges_and_tunnels_in_New_York_City "Bridges and tunnels in New York City"), and [skyscrapers](/wiki/Tallest_buildings_in_New_York_City "Tallest buildings in New York City") including the [Empire State Building](/wiki/Empire_State_Building "Empire State Building"), [Chrysler Building](/wiki/Chrysler_Building "Chrysler Building"), and [One World Trade Center](/wiki/One_World_Trade_Center "One World Trade Center").[[28]](#cite_note-30) It is also home to the [National Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association")'s [New York Knicks](/wiki/New_York_Knicks "New York Knicks") and the [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League")'s [New York Rangers](/wiki/New_York_Rangers "New York Rangers").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Manhattan&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Manhattan](/wiki/History_of_Manhattan "History of Manhattan")

See also: [History of New York City](/wiki/History_of_New_York_City "History of New York City")

### Lenape settlement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Manhattan&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Lenape settlement")]

Main article: [Lenape settlements](/wiki/Lenape_settlements "Lenape settlements")

Manhattan was historically part of the [Lenapehoking](/wiki/Lenapehoking "Lenapehoking") territory inhabited by the [Munsee](/wiki/Munsee "Munsee"), [Lenape](/wiki/Lenape "Lenape"),[[29]](#cite_note-31) and [Wappinger](/wiki/Wappinger "Wappinger") [tribes](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States").[[30]](#cite_note-nb-32) There were several Lenape settlements in the area including [Sapohanikan](/wiki/Sapohanikan "Sapohanikan"), [Nechtanc](/wiki/Nechtanc "Nechtanc"), and [Konaande Kongh](/wiki/Konaande_Kongh "Konaande Kongh"), which were interconnected by a series of trails. The primary trail on the island, which would later become [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_(Manhattan) "Broadway (Manhattan)"), ran from what is now [Inwood](/wiki/Inwood,_Manhattan "Inwood, Manhattan") in the north to [Battery Park](/wiki/Battery_Park "Battery Park") in the south.[[31]](#cite_note-33) There were various sites for fishing and planting established by the Lenape throughout Manhattan.[[13]](#cite_note-:1-15)

#### Toponymy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Manhattan&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Toponymy")]

Further information: [Etymology of Manhattan](/wiki/Etymology_of_Manhattan "Etymology of Manhattan")

The [etymology](/wiki/Etymology "Etymology") of the name *Manhattan* is most likely – amongst other theories, and via [loaning](/wiki/Loanword "Loanword") by [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language") — from the [Lenape](/wiki/Lenape "Lenape")'s local language [Munsee](/wiki/Munsee_language "Munsee language"), *manaháhtaan* (where *manah-* means "gather", *-aht-* means "[bow](/wiki/Bow_(archery) "Bow (archery)")", and *-aan* is used to form verb [stems](/wiki/Word_stem "Word stem")). The Lenape word has been translated as "the place where we get bows" or "place for gathering the (wood to make) bows". According to a Munsee tradition recorded by [Albert Seqaqkind Anthony](/wiki/Albert_Seqaqkind_Anthony "Albert Seqaqkind Anthony") in the 19th century, the island was named for a grove of [hickory trees](/wiki/Hickory_tree "Hickory tree") that was considered ideal for bowmaking. An alternate theory claims a "[Delaware](/wiki/Delaware_languages "Delaware languages") source akin to [Munsee](/wiki/Munsee "Munsee") *munahan* ("island")."[[32]](#cite_note-34)[[33]](#cite_note-35)

### Colonial era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Manhattan&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Colonial era")]

Main articles: [New Netherland](/wiki/New_Netherland "New Netherland"), [New Amsterdam](/wiki/New_Amsterdam "New Amsterdam"), and [Province of New York](/wiki/Province_of_New_York "Province of New York")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Stad_Amsterdam_in_Nieuw_Nederland_%28City_Amsterdam_in_New_Netherland%29_Castello_Plan_1660.jpg/250px-Stad_Amsterdam_in_Nieuw_Nederland_%28City_Amsterdam_in_New_Netherland%29_Castello_Plan_1660.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stad_Amsterdam_in_Nieuw_Nederland_(City_Amsterdam_in_New_Netherland)_Castello_Plan_1660.jpg)

The [Castello Plan](/wiki/Castello_Plan "Castello Plan"), a 1660 map of New Amsterdam (the top right corner is roughly north) in [Lower Manhattan](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan "Lower Manhattan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/GezichtOpNieuwAmsterdam.jpg/330px-GezichtOpNieuwAmsterdam.jpg)](/wiki/File:GezichtOpNieuwAmsterdam.jpg)

[New Amsterdam](/wiki/New_Amsterdam "New Amsterdam"), centered in what eventually became Lower Manhattan, in 1664, the year [England](/wiki/British_colonization_of_the_Americas "British colonization of the Americas") took control and renamed it New York

In April 1524, [Florentine](/wiki/Florence "Florence") explorer [Giovanni da Verrazzano](/wiki/Giovanni_da_Verrazzano "Giovanni da Verrazzano"), sailing in service of [Francis I of France](/wiki/Francis_I_of_France "Francis I of France"), became the first documented European to visit the area that would become New York City.[[34]](#cite_note-36) Verrazzano entered the [tidal strait](/wiki/Tidal_strait "Tidal strait") now known as [The Narrows](/wiki/The_Narrows "The Narrows") and named the land around [Upper New York Harbor](/wiki/Upper_New_York_Harbor "Upper New York Harbor") *[New Angoulême](/wiki/New_Angoul%C3%AAme "New Angoulême")*, in reference to the family name of [King Francis I](/wiki/King_Francis_I "King Francis I"); he sailed far enough into the harbor to sight the [Hudson River](/wiki/Hudson_River "Hudson River"), and he named the *Bay of Santa Margarita* – what is now [Upper New York Bay](/wiki/Upper_New_York_Bay "Upper New York Bay") – after [Marguerite de Navarre](/wiki/Marguerite_de_Navarre "Marguerite de Navarre"), the elder sister of the king.[[35]](#cite_note-37)[[36]](#cite_note-38)

Manhattan was first mapped during a 1609 voyage of [Henry Hudson](/wiki/Henry_Hudson "Henry Hudson").[[37]](#cite_note-39) Hudson came across Manhattan Island and the native people living there, and continued up the river that would later bear his name, the [Hudson River](/wiki/Hudson_River "Hudson River").[[38]](#cite_note-SciAm-40) Manhattan was first recorded in writing as *Manna-hata*, in the logbook of Robert Juet, an officer on the voyage.[[39]](#cite_note-41)

A permanent European presence in [New Netherland](/wiki/New_Netherland "New Netherland") began in 1624, with the founding of a [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic") [fur trading](/wiki/Fur_trading "Fur trading") settlement on [Governors Island](/wiki/Governors_Island "Governors Island").[[40]](#cite_note-42) In 1625, construction was started on the [citadel](/wiki/Citadel "Citadel") of [Fort Amsterdam](/wiki/Fort_Amsterdam "Fort Amsterdam") on Manhattan Island, later called [New Amsterdam](/wiki/New_Amsterdam "New Amsterdam") (*Nieuw Amsterdam*), in what is now Lower Manhattan.[[41]](#cite_note-43)[[42]](#cite_note-Tolerance-44)[[43]](#cite_note-45) The establishment of Fort Amsterdam is recognized as the birth of New York City.[[44]](#cite_note-46)
In 1647, [Peter Stuyvesant](/wiki/Peter_Stuyvesant "Peter Stuyvesant") was appointed as the last Dutch Director-General of the colony.[[45]](#cite_note-47) New Amsterdam was formally incorporated as a city on February 2, 1653.[[46]](#cite_note-48) In 1664, English forces conquered New Netherland and renamed it "New York" after the English [Duke of York and Albany](/wiki/James_II_of_England "James II of England"), the future King James II.[[47]](#cite_note-49) In August 1673, the [Dutch reconquered](/wiki/Reconquest_of_New_Netherland "Reconquest of New Netherland") the colony, renaming it "New Orange", but permanently relinquished it back to England the following year under the terms of the [Treaty of Westminster](/wiki/Treaty_of_Westminster_(1674) "Treaty of Westminster (1674)") that ended the [Third Anglo-Dutch War](/wiki/Third_Anglo-Dutch_War "Third Anglo-Dutch War").[[48]](#cite_note-50)[[49]](#cite_note-51)

### American Revolution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Manhattan&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: American Revolution")]

Further information: [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/George_Washington_Statue_at_Federal_Hall.JPG/250px-George_Washington_Statue_at_Federal_Hall.JPG)](/wiki/File:George_Washington_Statue_at_Federal_Hall.JPG)

[Statue](/wiki/Statue_of_George_Washington_(Wall_Street) "Statue of George Washington (Wall Street)") of [George Washington](/wiki/George_Washington "George Washington") in front of [Federal Hall](/wiki/Federal_Hall "Federal Hall") on [Wall Street](/wiki/Wall_Street "Wall Street"), where in 1789 he was sworn in as the [first U.S. president](/wiki/Presidency_of_George_Washington "Presidency of George Washington").[[50]](#cite_note-52)

Manhattan was at the heart of the [New York Campaign](/wiki/New_York_Campaign "New York Campaign"), a series of major battles in the early stages of the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"). The [Continental Army](/wiki/Continental_Army "Continental Army") was forced to abandon Manhattan after the [Battle of Fort Washington](/wiki/Battle_of_Fort_Washington "Battle of Fort Washington") on November 16, 1776.[[51]](#cite_note-53) The city, greatly damaged by the [Great Fire of New York](/wiki/Great_Fire_of_New_York_(1776) "Great Fire of New York (1776)") during the campaign, became the British military and political center of operations in North America for the remainder of the war.[[52]](#cite_note-54) British occupation lasted until November 25, 1783, when [George Washington](/wiki/George_Washington "George Washington") returned to Manhattan, a day celebrated as [Evacuation Day](/wiki/Evacuation_Day_(New_York) "Evacuation Day (New York)"), marking when the last British forces left the city.[[53]](#cite_note-55)

From January 11, 1785, until 1789, New York City was the fifth of five [capitals of the United States](/wiki/Capitals_of_the_United_States "Capitals of the United States") under the [Articles of Confederation](/wiki/Articles_of_Confederation "Articles of Confederation"), with the [Continental Congress](/wiki/Continental_Congress "Continental Congress") meeting at [New York City Hall](/wiki/New_York_City_Hall "New York City Hall") (then at [Fraunces Tavern](/wiki/Fraunces_Tavern "Fraunces Tavern")).[[54]](#cite_note-56) New York was the first capital under the newly enacted [Constitution of the United States](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_United_States "Constitution of the United States"), from March 4, 1789, to August 12, 1790, at [Federal Hall](/wiki/Federal_Hall "Federal Hall").[[15]](#cite_note-senate-17) Federal Hall was where the [United States Supreme Court](/wiki/United_States_Supreme_Court "United States Supreme Court") met for the first time,[[55]](#cite_note-57) the [United States Bill of Rights](/wiki/United_States_Bill_of_Rights "United States Bill of Rights") were drafted and ratified,[[56]](#cite_note-cwf-58) and where the [Northwest Ordinance](/wiki/Northwest_Ordinance "Northwest Ordinance") was adopted, establishing measures for [admission to the Union](/wiki/Admission_to_the_Union "Admission to the Union") of new states.[[57]](#cite_note-59)

### 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Manhattan&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 19th century")]

New York grew as an economic center, first as a result of [Alexander Hamilton](/wiki/Alexander_Hamilton "Alexander Hamilton")'s policies and practices as the first [Secretary of the Treasury](/wiki/Secretary_of_the_Treasury "Secretary of the Treasury") to expand the city's role as a center of commerce and industry.[[58]](#cite_note-60) In 1810, New York City, then confined to Manhattan, had surpassed [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia") as the most populous city in the United States.[[59]](#cite_note-61) The [Commissioners' Plan of 1811](/wiki/Commissioners%27_Plan_of_1811 "Commissioners' Plan of 1811") laid out the island of Manhattan in its familiar [grid plan](/wiki/Grid_plan "Grid plan").[[60]](#cite_note-MCNY1811-62) The city's role as an economic center grew with the opening of the [Erie Canal](/wiki/Erie_Canal "Erie Canal") in 1825, cutting transportation costs by 90% compared to road transport and connecting the Atlantic port to the vast agricultural markets of the [Midwestern United States](/wiki/Midwestern_United_States "Midwestern United States") and Canada.[[61]](#cite_note-63)[[62]](#cite_note-lankevich-p67-64)[[63]](#cite_note-65)

[Tammany Hall](/wiki/Tammany_Hall "Tammany Hall"), a [Democratic Party](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") [political machine](/wiki/Political_machine "Political machine"), began to grow in influence with the support of many of the [immigrant Irish](/wiki/Irish_diaspora "Irish diaspora"), culminating in the election of the first Tammany mayor, [Fernando Wood](/wiki/Fernando_Wood "Fernando Wood"), in 1854.[[64]](#cite_note-66) Covering 840 acres (340 ha) in the center of the island, [Central Park](/wiki/Central_Park "Central Park"), which opened its first portions to the public in 1858, became the first [landscaped public park](/wiki/Arboriculture "Arboriculture") in an American city.[[65]](#cite_note-67)[[66]](#cite_note-68)[[67]](#cite_note-69)[[68]](#cite_note-70)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/Viele_Map_1865.jpg/960px-Viele_Map_1865.jpg)](/wiki/File:Viele_Map_1865.jpg "The \"Sanitary & Topographical Map of the City and Island of New York\", commonly known as the Viele Map, developed by Egbert Ludovicus Viele in 1865")

The "Sanitary & Topographical Map of the City and Island of New York", commonly known as the Viele Map, developed by [Egbert Ludovicus Viele](/wiki/Egbert_Ludovicus_Viele "Egbert Ludovicus Viele") in 1865

New York City played a complex role in the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"). The city had strong commercial ties to the [South](/wiki/Southern_United_States "Southern United States"), but anger around [conscription](/wiki/Conscription "Conscription"), resentment against Lincoln's war policies and paranoia about [free Blacks](/wiki/Free_Blacks "Free Blacks") taking the jobs of poor immigrants[[69]](#cite_note-71) culminated in the three-day-long [New York Draft riots](/wiki/New_York_Draft_riots "New York Draft riots") of July 1863, among the worst incidents of [civil disorder](/wiki/Civil_disorder "Civil disorder") in American history.[[70]](#cite_note-72) The rate of immigration from Europe grew steeply after the Civil War, and Manhattan became the first stop for millions seeking a new life in the United States, a role acknowledged by the dedication of the [Statue of Liberty](/wiki/Statue_of_Liberty "Statue of Liberty") in 1886.[[71]](#cite_note-73)[[72]](#cite_note-74) This immigration brought further social upheaval. In a city of tenements packed with poorly paid laborers from dozens of nations, the city became a hotbed of revolution (including anarchists and [communists](/wiki/Communist "Communist") among others), [syndicalism](/wiki/Syndicalism "Syndicalism"), [racketeering](/wiki/Racketeering "Racketeering"), and [unionization](/wiki/Unionization "Unionization").[[73]](#cite_note-75)

In 1883, the opening of the [Brooklyn Bridge](/wiki/Brooklyn_Bridge "Brooklyn Bridge") across the [East River](/wiki/East_River "East River") established a road connection to [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn") and the rest of [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island").[[74]](#cite_note-76) In 1898, New York City consolidated with three neighboring counties to form "the [City of Greater New York](/wiki/City_of_Greater_New_York "City of Greater New York")", and Manhattan was established as one of the five [boroughs of New York City](/wiki/Boroughs_of_New_York_City "Boroughs of New York City").[[75]](#cite_note-77)[[76]](#cite_note-78) [The Bronx](/wiki/The_Bronx "The Bronx") remained part of New York County until 1914, when Bronx County was established.[[77]](#cite_note-79)

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Manhattan&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 20th century")]

Further information: [Triangle Shirtwaist Factory fire](/wiki/Triangle_Shirtwaist_Factory_fire "Triangle Shirtwaist Factory fire") and [Stonewall riots](/wiki/Stonewall_riots "Stonewall riots")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Mulberry_Street_NYC_c1900_LOC_3g04637u_edit.jpg/250px-Mulberry_Street_NYC_c1900_LOC_3g04637u_edit.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mulberry_Street_NYC_c1900_LOC_3g04637u_edit.jpg)

Manhattan's [Little Italy](/wiki/Little_Italy,_Manhattan "Little Italy, Manhattan") on the [Lower East Side](/wiki/Lower_East_Side "Lower East Side"), c. 1900

[![A man working on a steel girder high above a city skyline.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/Old_timer_structural_worker2.jpg/250px-Old_timer_structural_worker2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Old_timer_structural_worker2.jpg)

A [construction worker](/wiki/Construction_worker "Construction worker") atop the [Empire State Building](/wiki/Empire_State_Building "Empire State Building") during its construction in 1930. The [Chrysler Building](/wiki/Chrysler_Building "Chrysler Building") is visible to the right.

[![A two-story building with brick on the first floor, with two arched doorways, and gray stucco on the second floor off of which hang numerous rainbow flags.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Stonewall_Inn_5_pride_weekend_2016.jpg/250px-Stonewall_Inn_5_pride_weekend_2016.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stonewall_Inn_5_pride_weekend_2016.jpg)

[Stonewall Inn](/wiki/Stonewall_Inn "Stonewall Inn") in [Greenwich Village](/wiki/Greenwich_Village "Greenwich Village"), the site of the June 1969 [Stonewall riots](/wiki/Stonewall_riots "Stonewall riots") and the cradle of the modern [LGBTQ+ rights](/wiki/LGBTQ%2B_rights "LGBTQ+ rights") movement

The construction of the [New York City Subway](/wiki/New_York_City_Subway "New York City Subway"), which opened in 1904, helped bind the new city together,[[78]](#cite_note-80) as did the completion of the [Williamsburg Bridge](/wiki/Williamsburg_Bridge "Williamsburg Bridge") (1903) and [Manhattan Bridge](/wiki/Manhattan_Bridge "Manhattan Bridge") (1909) connecting to Brooklyn and the [Queensboro Bridge](/wiki/Queensboro_Bridge "Queensboro Bridge") (1909) connecting to Queens.[[79]](#cite_note-81) In the 1920s, Manhattan experienced large arrivals of African-Americans as part of the [Great Migration](/wiki/Great_Migration_(African_American) "Great Migration (African American)") from the southern United States, and the [Harlem Renaissance](/wiki/Harlem_Renaissance "Harlem Renaissance"),[[80]](#cite_note-82) part of a larger boom time in the [Prohibition](/wiki/Prohibition_in_the_United_States "Prohibition in the United States") era that included new skyscrapers competing for the skyline, with the [Woolworth Building](/wiki/Woolworth_Building "Woolworth Building") (1913), [40 Wall Street](/wiki/40_Wall_Street "40 Wall Street") (1930), the [Chrysler Building](/wiki/Chrysler_Building "Chrysler Building") (1930), and the [Empire State Building](/wiki/Empire_State_Building "Empire State Building") (1931) leapfrogging each other to take their place as the [world's tallest building](/wiki/History_of_the_world%27s_tallest_buildings "History of the world's tallest buildings").[[81]](#cite_note-83)

Manhattan's majority [white](/wiki/White_American "White American") ethnic group declined from 98.7% in 1900 to 58.3% by 1990.[[82]](#cite_note-Census1790to1990-84) On March 25, 1911, the [Triangle Shirtwaist Factory fire](/wiki/Triangle_Shirtwaist_Factory_fire "Triangle Shirtwaist Factory fire") in [Greenwich Village](/wiki/Greenwich_Village "Greenwich Village") killed 146 [garment workers](/wiki/Garment_worker "Garment worker"),[[83]](#cite_note-85) leading to overhauls of the city's fire department, [building codes](/wiki/Building_code "Building code"), and workplace safety regulations.[[84]](#cite_note-86) In 1912, about 20,000 workers, a quarter of them women, marched upon [Washington Square Park](/wiki/Washington_Square_Park "Washington Square Park") to commemorate the fire. Many of the women wore fitted tucked-front blouses like those manufactured by the company, a clothing style that became the working woman's uniform and a symbol of [women's liberation](/wiki/Women%27s_liberation "Women's liberation"), reflecting the alliance of the labor and [suffrage](/wiki/Suffrage "Suffrage") movements.[[85]](#cite_note-87)

Despite the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression"), some of the world's tallest skyscrapers were completed in Manhattan during the 1930s, including numerous [Art Deco](/wiki/Art_Deco "Art Deco") masterpieces that are still part of the city's skyline, most notably the Empire State Building, the Chrysler Building, and [30 Rockefeller Plaza](/wiki/30_Rockefeller_Plaza "30 Rockefeller Plaza").[[86]](#cite_note-88) A postwar economic boom led to the development of huge housing developments targeted at returning veterans, the largest being [Stuyvesant Town–Peter Cooper Village](/wiki/Stuyvesant_Town%E2%80%93Peter_Cooper_Village "Stuyvesant Town–Peter Cooper Village"), which opened in 1947.[[87]](#cite_note-89)[[88]](#cite_note-90) The United Nations relocated to a new [headquarters](/wiki/Headquarters_of_the_United_Nations "Headquarters of the United Nations") that was completed in 1952 along the East River.[[89]](#cite_note-91)[[90]](#cite_note-92)[[91]](#cite_note-93)

The [Stonewall riots](/wiki/Stonewall_riots "Stonewall riots") were a series of spontaneous, violent protests by members of the [gay community](/wiki/Gay_community "Gay community") against a [police raid](/wiki/Police_raid "Police raid") that took place in the early morning hours of June 28, 1969, at the [Stonewall Inn](/wiki/Stonewall_Inn "Stonewall Inn") in the [Greenwich Village](/wiki/Greenwich_Village "Greenwich Village") neighborhood of Lower Manhattan. They are widely considered to constitute the single most important event leading to the [gay liberation](/wiki/Gay_liberation "Gay liberation") movement[[92]](#cite_note-GayGreenwichVillage1-94)[[93]](#cite_note-KentuckyStonewall-95) and the modern fight for [LGBT rights](/wiki/LGBT_rights "LGBT rights").[[94]](#cite_note-NPSStonewall-96)[[19]](#cite_note-ObamaStonewall-21)[[95]](#cite_note-GayborhoodGreenwichVillage-97)[[18]](#cite_note-NPS99000562-20)[[96]](#cite_note-98) In the 1970s, job losses due to [industrial restructuring](/wiki/Deindustrialization "Deindustrialization") caused New York City, including Manhattan, to suffer from economic problems and rising crime rates.[[97]](#cite_note-99) While a resurgence in the [financial industry](/wiki/Financial_industry "Financial industry") greatly improved the city's economic health in the 1980s, New York's crime rate continued to increase through the decade and into the beginning of the 1990s.[[98]](#cite_note-100) The 1980s saw a rebirth of [Wall Street](/wiki/Wall_Street "Wall Street"), and Manhattan reclaimed its role as the world's [financial center](/wiki/Financial_center "Financial center"), with Wall Street employment doubling from 1977 to 1987.[[99]](#cite_note-101) The 1980s also saw Manhattan at the heart of the [AIDS crisis](/wiki/AIDS_crisis "AIDS crisis"), with Greenwich Village at its epicenter.[[100]](#cite_note-102)

In the 1970s, [Times Square](/wiki/Times_Square "Times Square") and [42nd Street](/wiki/42nd_Street_(Manhattan) "42nd Street (Manhattan)") – with its sex shops, [peep shows](/wiki/Peep_show "Peep show"), and adult theaters, along with its [sex trade](/wiki/Prostitution "Prostitution"), street crime, and public drug use – became emblematic of the city's decline, with a 1981 article in *[Rolling Stone](/wiki/Rolling_Stone "Rolling Stone")* magazine calling the stretch of West 42nd Street between [7th](/wiki/7th_Avenue_(Manhattan) "7th Avenue (Manhattan)") and [8th Avenues](/wiki/8th_Avenue_(Manhattan) "8th Avenue (Manhattan)") the "sleaziest block in America".[[101]](#cite_note-103) By the late 1990s, led by efforts by the city and the [Walt Disney Company](/wiki/Walt_Disney_Company "Walt Disney Company"), the area had been revived as a center of tourism to the point where it was described by *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* as "arguably the most sought-after 13 acres of commercial property in the world."[[102]](#cite_note-104)

By the 1990s, crime rates began to drop dramatically[[103]](#cite_note-105)[[104]](#cite_note-106) and the city once again became the destination of immigrants from around the world, joining with low interest rates and Wall Street [bonus payments](/wiki/Bonus_payment "Bonus payment") to fuel the growth of the real estate market.[[105]](#cite_note-107) Important new sectors, such as [Silicon Alley](/wiki/Silicon_Alley "Silicon Alley"), emerged in the [Flatiron District](/wiki/Flatiron_District "Flatiron District"), cementing technology as a key component of Manhattan's economy.[[106]](#cite_note-108) The [1993 World Trade Center bombing](/wiki/1993_World_Trade_Center_bombing "1993 World Trade Center bombing"), described by the [FBI](/wiki/FBI "FBI") as "something of a deadly dress rehearsal for 9/11", was a terrorist attack in which six people were killed when a van bomb filled with explosives was detonated in a parking lot below the [North Tower](/wiki/List_of_tenants_in_1_World_Trade_Center_(1971%E2%80%932001) "List of tenants in 1 World Trade Center (1971–2001)") of the [World Trade Center complex](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_(1973%E2%80%932001) "World Trade Center (1973–2001)").[[107]](#cite_note-109)

### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Manhattan&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 21st century")]

See also: [September 11 attacks](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/UA_Flight_175_hits_WTC_south_tower_9-11_edit.jpeg/250px-UA_Flight_175_hits_WTC_south_tower_9-11_edit.jpeg)](/wiki/File:UA_Flight_175_hits_WTC_south_tower_9-11_edit.jpeg)

[United Airlines Flight 175](/wiki/United_Airlines_Flight_175 "United Airlines Flight 175") hits the [South Tower](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_(1973%E2%80%932001) "World Trade Center (1973–2001)") on September 11, 2001.

On September 11, 2001, the [Twin Towers of the original World Trade Center](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_(1973%E2%80%932001) "World Trade Center (1973–2001)") were struck by hijacked aircraft and collapsed in the [September 11 attacks](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks") launched by [al-Qaeda](/wiki/Al-Qaeda "Al-Qaeda") terrorists. The collapse caused extensive damage to surrounding buildings and skyscrapers in Lower Manhattan, and resulted in the [deaths of 2,606](/wiki/Casualties_of_the_September_11_attacks "Casualties of the September 11 attacks") of the 17,400 who had been in the buildings when the planes hit, in addition to those on the planes.[[108]](#cite_note-110) Since 2001, most of [Lower Manhattan](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan "Lower Manhattan") has been restored, although [there has been controversy](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_rebuilding_controversy "World Trade Center rebuilding controversy") surrounding the rebuilding. In 2014, the new [One World Trade Center](/wiki/One_World_Trade_Center "One World Trade Center"), at 1,776 feet (541 m) measured to the top of its spire, became the tallest building in the Western Hemisphere[[109]](#cite_note-111) and is the [world's seventh-tallest building (as of 2023)](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings "List of tallest buildings").[[110]](#cite_note-112)

The [Occupy Wall Street](/wiki/Occupy_Wall_Street "Occupy Wall Street") protests in [Zuccotti Park](/wiki/Zuccotti_Park "Zuccotti Park") in the [Financial District](/wiki/Financial_District_(Manhattan) "Financial District (Manhattan)") of Lower Manhattan began on September 17, 2011, receiving global attention and spawning the [Occupy movement](/wiki/Occupy_movement "Occupy movement") against [social](/wiki/Social_inequality "Social inequality") and [economic inequality](/wiki/Economic_inequality "Economic inequality") worldwide.[[111]](#cite_note-113)[[112]](#cite_note-114)

On October 29 and 30, 2012, [Hurricane Sandy](/wiki/Hurricane_Sandy "Hurricane Sandy") caused [extensive destruction](/wiki/Effects_of_Hurricane_Sandy_in_New_York "Effects of Hurricane Sandy in New York") in the borough, ravaging portions of Lower Manhattan with record-high [storm surge](/wiki/Storm_surge "Storm surge") from New York Harbor,[[113]](#cite_note-NYC_after_Sandy-115) severe flooding, and high winds, causing [power outages](/wiki/Power_outage "Power outage") for hundreds of thousands of city residents[[114]](#cite_note-116) and leading to gasoline shortages[[115]](#cite_note-117) and disruption of [mass transit](/wiki/Mass_transit "Mass transit") systems.[[116]](#cite_note-118)[[117]](#cite_note-119)[[118]](#cite_note-120)[[119]](#cite_note-121) The storm and its profound impacts have prompted discussion of constructing [seawalls](/wiki/Seawall "Seawall") and other [coastal barriers](/wiki/Coastal_management "Coastal management") around the [shorelines](/wiki/Shoreline "Shoreline") of the borough and the metropolitan area to minimize the risk of destructive consequences from another such event in the future.[[120]](#cite_note-122)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Manhattan&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Geography")]

See also: [Geography of New York City](/wiki/Geography_of_New_York_City "Geography of New York City")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Manhattan_by_Sentinel-2.jpg/330px-Manhattan_by_Sentinel-2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Manhattan_by_Sentinel-2.jpg)

A satellite image of Manhattan, bounded by the [Hudson River](/wiki/Hudson_River "Hudson River") to the west, the [Harlem River](/wiki/Harlem_River "Harlem River") to the north, the [East River](/wiki/East_River "East River") to the east, and [New York Harbor](/wiki/New_York_Harbor "New York Harbor") to the south, with rectangular [Central Park](/wiki/Central_Park "Central Park") prominently visible. [Roosevelt Island](/wiki/Roosevelt_Island "Roosevelt Island"), in the East River, belongs to Manhattan.

According to the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), New York County has a total area of 33.6 square miles (87 km2), of which 22.8 square miles (59 km2) is land and 10.8 square miles (28 km2) (32%) is water.[[2]](#cite_note-CensusArea-2) The northern segment of Upper Manhattan represents a geographic [panhandle](/wiki/Panhandle "Panhandle"). Manhattan Island is 22.7 square miles (59 km2) in area, 13.4 miles (21.6 km) long and 2.3 miles (3.7 km) wide, at its widest point, near [14th Street](/wiki/14th_Street_(Manhattan) "14th Street (Manhattan)").[[4]](#cite_note-Passikoff-4)[[121]](#cite_note-123)

The borough consists primarily of Manhattan Island, along with the [Marble Hill](/wiki/Marble_Hill,_Manhattan "Marble Hill, Manhattan") neighborhood and several small islands, including [Randalls Island and Wards Island](/wiki/Randalls_and_Wards_Islands "Randalls and Wards Islands") and [Roosevelt Island](/wiki/Roosevelt_Island "Roosevelt Island") in the East River; and [Governors Island](/wiki/Governors_Island "Governors Island") and [Liberty Island](/wiki/Liberty_Island "Liberty Island") to the south in [New York Harbor](/wiki/New_York_Harbor "New York Harbor").[[122]](#cite_note-Islands-124)

### Manhattan Island

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Manhattan&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Manhattan Island")]

The Island is about 13.4 miles (21.6 km) from north to south, and at its widest, 2.3 miles (3.7 km).[[4]](#cite_note-Passikoff-4) Manhattan Island is loosely divided into Downtown ([Lower Manhattan](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan "Lower Manhattan")), Midtown ([Midtown Manhattan](/wiki/Midtown_Manhattan "Midtown Manhattan")), and Uptown ([Upper Manhattan](/wiki/Upper_Manhattan "Upper Manhattan")), with [Fifth Avenue](/wiki/Fifth_Avenue "Fifth Avenue") dividing Manhattan lengthwise into its [East Side](/wiki/East_Side_(Manhattan) "East Side (Manhattan)") and [West Side](/wiki/West_Side_(Manhattan) "West Side (Manhattan)").[[123]](#cite_note-125) Manhattan Island is bounded by the [Hudson River](/wiki/Hudson_River "Hudson River") to the west and the [East River](/wiki/East_River "East River") to the east. To the north, the [Harlem River](/wiki/Harlem_River "Harlem River") divides Manhattan Island from [the Bronx](/wiki/The_Bronx "The Bronx") and the mainland United States.[[124]](#cite_note-126)

Early in the 19th century, [land reclamation](/wiki/Land_reclamation "Land reclamation") was used to expand Lower Manhattan from the natural Hudson shoreline at [Greenwich Street](/wiki/Greenwich_Street "Greenwich Street") to [West Street](/wiki/West_Street "West Street").[[125]](#cite_note-127) When [building the World Trade Center](/wiki/Building_the_World_Trade_Center "Building the World Trade Center") in 1968, 1.2 million cubic yards (920,000 m3) of material excavated from the site[[126]](#cite_note-128) was used to expand the Manhattan [shoreline](/wiki/Shoreline "Shoreline") across West Street, creating [Battery Park City](/wiki/Battery_Park_City "Battery Park City").[[127]](#cite_note-129) Constructed on piers at a cost of $260 million, [Little Island](/wiki/Little_Island_at_Pier_55 "Little Island at Pier 55") opened on the Hudson River in May 2021, connected to the western termini of [13th](/wiki/13th_Street_(Manhattan) "13th Street (Manhattan)") and [14th Streets](/wiki/14th_Street_(Manhattan) "14th Street (Manhattan)") by [footbridges](/wiki/Footbridge "Footbridge").[[128]](#cite_note-130)

### Marble Hill

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Manhattan&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Marble Hill")]

Main article: [Marble Hill, Manhattan](/wiki/Marble_Hill,_Manhattan "Marble Hill, Manhattan")

Marble Hill was part of the northern tip of Manhattan Island, but the [Harlem River Ship Canal](/wiki/Harlem_River_Ship_Canal "Harlem River Ship Canal"), dug in 1895 to better connect the [Harlem](/wiki/Harlem_River "Harlem River") and [Hudson](/wiki/Hudson_River "Hudson River") rivers, separated it from the remainder of Manhattan.[[129]](#cite_note-canal-131) Before World War I, the section of the original Harlem River channel separating Marble Hill from the Bronx was filled in, and Marble Hill became part of the mainland.[[130]](#cite_note-132) After a May 1984 court ruling that Marble Hill was simultaneously part of the Borough of Manhattan (not the Borough of the Bronx) and part of Bronx County (not New York County),[[131]](#cite_note-133) the matter was definitively settled later that year when the [New York Legislature](/wiki/New_York_Legislature "New York Legislature") overwhelmingly passed legislation declaring the neighborhood part of both New York County and the Borough of Manhattan.[[132]](#cite_note-134)[[133]](#cite_note-135)

### Smaller islands

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Manhattan&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Smaller islands")]

See also: [List of smaller islands in New York City](/wiki/List_of_smaller_islands_in_New_York_City "List of smaller islands in New York City")

[![A tall green statue on an island in a harbor.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Liberty_Island_photo_Don_Ramey_Logan.jpg/250px-Liberty_Island_photo_Don_Ramey_Logan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Liberty_Island_photo_Don_Ramey_Logan.jpg)

[Liberty Island](/wiki/Liberty_Island "Liberty Island"), an [exclave](/wiki/Exclave "Exclave") of Manhattan, New York City, and the [state of New York](/wiki/State_of_New_York "State of New York"), that is surrounded by [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey") waters

Within [New York Harbor](/wiki/New_York_Harbor "New York Harbor"), there are three smaller islands:

* [Ellis Island](/wiki/Ellis_Island "Ellis Island"), shared with [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey")[[134]](#cite_note-136)
* [Governors Island](/wiki/Governors_Island "Governors Island")[[135]](#cite_note-137)
* [Liberty Island](/wiki/Liberty_Island "Liberty Island") (administered by the [National Park Service](/wiki/National_Park_Service "National Park Service"))[[136]](#cite_note-138)

Other smaller islands, in the [East River](/wiki/East_River "East River"), include (from north to south):

* [Randalls and Wards Islands](/wiki/Randalls_and_Wards_Islands "Randalls and Wards Islands"), joined by landfill
* [Mill Rock](/wiki/Mill_Rock "Mill Rock")
* [Roosevelt Island](/wiki/Roosevelt_Island "Roosevelt Island"), which has a population of 14,000, extends for 2 miles (3.2 km), and was renamed in 1973 from Welfare Island to honor President [Franklin D. Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Franklin D. Roosevelt").[[137]](#cite_note-139)
* [U Thant Island](/wiki/U_Thant_Island "U Thant Island") (legally Belmont Island)

### Geology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Manhattan&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Geology")]

Main article: [Geology of Manhattan](/wiki/Geology_of_Manhattan "Geology of Manhattan")

The [bedrock](/wiki/Bedrock "Bedrock") underlying much of Manhattan consists of three rock formations: *Inwood [marble](/wiki/Marble "Marble")*, *Fordham [gneiss](/wiki/Gneiss "Gneiss")*, and *Manhattan [schist](/wiki/Schist "Schist")*, and is well suited for the foundations of Manhattan's [skyscrapers](/wiki/Skyscrapers "Skyscrapers").[[138]](#cite_note-140) It is part of the [Manhattan Prong](/wiki/Manhattan_Prong "Manhattan Prong") physiographic region.

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Manhattan&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Climate")]

See also: [Climate of New York City](/wiki/Climate_of_New_York_City "Climate of New York City")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/Southwest_corner_of_Central_Park%2C_looking_east%2C_NYC.jpg/250px-Southwest_corner_of_Central_Park%2C_looking_east%2C_NYC.jpg)](/wiki/File:Southwest_corner_of_Central_Park,_looking_east,_NYC.jpg)

[Central Park](/wiki/Central_Park "Central Park") in autumn

Under the [Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"), New York City features both a [humid subtropical climate](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate") (*Cfa*) and a [humid continental climate](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate") (*Dfa*).[[139]](#cite_note-141) It is the northernmost major city on the North American continent with a humid subtropical climate. The city averages 234 days with at least some sunshine annually.[[140]](#cite_note-noaasun-142)

Winters are cold and damp, and prevailing wind patterns that blow offshore temper the moderating effects of the Atlantic Ocean. The Atlantic and the partial shielding from colder air by the [Appalachians](/wiki/Appalachians "Appalachians") keep the city warmer in the winter than inland North American cities at similar or lesser latitudes. The daily mean temperature in January, the area's coldest month, is 32.6 °F (0.3 °C).[[141]](#cite_note-New_York_City_Weatherbox_NOAA_txt-143) Temperatures usually drop to 10 °F (−12 °C) several times per winter,[[141]](#cite_note-New_York_City_Weatherbox_NOAA_txt-143)[[142]](#cite_note-NYC_climate-144) and reach 60 °F (16 °C) several days in the coldest winter month.[[141]](#cite_note-New_York_City_Weatherbox_NOAA_txt-143) Spring and autumn are unpredictable and can range from chilly to warm, although they are usually mild with low humidity. Summers are typically warm to hot and humid, with a daily mean temperature of 76.5 °F (24.7 °C) in July.[[141]](#cite_note-New_York_City_Weatherbox_NOAA_txt-143)

Nighttime conditions are often exacerbated by the [urban heat island](/wiki/Urban_heat_island "Urban heat island") phenomenon, which causes heat absorbed during the day to be radiated back at night, raising temperatures by as much as 7 °F (4 °C) when winds are slow.[[143]](#cite_note-145) Daytime temperatures exceed 90 °F (32 °C) on average of 17 days each summer[[144]](#cite_note-New_York_City_Weatherbox_NOAA-146) and in some years exceed 100 °F (38 °C). Extreme temperatures have ranged from −15 °F (−26 °C), recorded on February 9, 1934, up to 106 °F (41 °C) on July 9, 1936.[[144]](#cite_note-New_York_City_Weatherbox_NOAA-146) Manhattan lies in USDA [plant hardiness zone](/wiki/Plant_hardiness_zone "Plant hardiness zone") 7b (5 to 10 °F/-15 to -12.2 °C).[[145]](#cite_note-147)

Manhattan receives 49.9 inches (1,270 mm) of [precipitation](/wiki/Precipitation "Precipitation") annually, which is relatively evenly spread throughout the year. Average winter snowfall between 1981 and 2010 has been 25.8 inches (66 cm). This varies considerably from year to year.[[144]](#cite_note-New_York_City_Weatherbox_NOAA-146)

### Neighborhoods

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Manhattan&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Neighborhoods")]

Main articles: [Neighborhoods in New York City](/wiki/Neighborhoods_in_New_York_City "Neighborhoods in New York City") and [List of Manhattan neighborhoods](/wiki/List_of_Manhattan_neighborhoods "List of Manhattan neighborhoods")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/Empire_State_Building_with_1WTC_in_background.png/250px-Empire_State_Building_with_1WTC_in_background.png)](/wiki/File:Empire_State_Building_with_1WTC_in_background.png)

The [Empire State Building](/wiki/Empire_State_Building "Empire State Building") in the foreground, looking south from the top of [Rockefeller Center](/wiki/Rockefeller_Center "Rockefeller Center") with [One World Trade Center](/wiki/One_World_Trade_Center "One World Trade Center") in the background

Manhattan's many neighborhoods are not named according to any particular convention, nor do they have official boundaries. Some are geographical (the [Upper East Side](/wiki/Upper_East_Side "Upper East Side")), or ethnically descriptive ([Little Italy](/wiki/Little_Italy,_Manhattan "Little Italy, Manhattan")). Others are [acronyms](/wiki/Acronyms "Acronyms"), such as [TriBeCa](/wiki/TriBeCa "TriBeCa") (for "TRIangle BElow CAnal Street") or [SoHo](/wiki/SoHo "SoHo") ("SOuth of HOuston"), [NoLIta](/wiki/NoLIta "NoLIta") ("NOrth of Little ITAly"), and [NoMad](/wiki/NoMad "NoMad") ("NOrth of MADison Square Park").[[148]](#cite_note-152)[[149]](#cite_note-153)[[150]](#cite_note-154)[[151]](#cite_note-155)[[152]](#cite_note-156) [Harlem](/wiki/Harlem "Harlem") is a name from the Dutch colonial era after [Haarlem](/wiki/Haarlem "Haarlem"), a city in the Netherlands.[[153]](#cite_note-157) Some have simple [folkloric](/wiki/Folkloric "Folkloric") names, such as [Hell's Kitchen](/wiki/Hell%27s_Kitchen "Hell's Kitchen"), alongside their more official but lesser used title (in this case, Clinton).[[154]](#cite_note-158)

Some neighborhoods, such as [SoHo](/wiki/SoHo "SoHo"), which is mixed use, are known for [upscale shopping](/wiki/Upscale_shopping "Upscale shopping") as well as residential use.[[155]](#cite_note-159) Others, such as [Greenwich Village](/wiki/Greenwich_Village "Greenwich Village"), the [Lower East Side](/wiki/Lower_East_Side "Lower East Side"), [Alphabet City](/wiki/Alphabet_City "Alphabet City") and the [East Village](/wiki/East_Village,_Manhattan "East Village, Manhattan"), have long been associated with the [Bohemian](/wiki/Bohemianism "Bohemianism") subculture.[[156]](#cite_note-160)[[157]](#cite_note-161)[[158]](#cite_note-162) [Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea,_Manhattan "Chelsea, Manhattan") is one of several Manhattan neighborhoods with large [gay populations](/wiki/LGBT_culture_in_New_York_City "LGBT culture in New York City") and has become a center of both the international [art industry](/wiki/Visual_art "Visual art") and New York's nightlife.[[159]](#cite_note-163) [Chinatown](/wiki/Chinatown,_Manhattan "Chinatown, Manhattan") has the highest concentration of people of [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_Americans_in_New_York_City "Chinese Americans in New York City") descent outside of Asia.[[160]](#cite_note-164)[[161]](#cite_note-165)

[Koreatown](/wiki/Koreatown,_Manhattan "Koreatown, Manhattan") is roughly centered on 32nd Street between Fifth and Sixth Avenues.[[162]](#cite_note-KoreatownExpanding-166) [Rose Hill](/wiki/Rose_Hill,_Manhattan "Rose Hill, Manhattan") features a growing number of Indian restaurants and spice shops along a stretch of [Lexington Avenue](/wiki/Lexington_Avenue "Lexington Avenue") between 25th and 30th Streets which has become known as *[Curry Hill](/wiki/Curry_Hill "Curry Hill")*.[[163]](#cite_note-167) [Washington Heights](/wiki/Washington_Heights,_Manhattan "Washington Heights, Manhattan") in [Uptown Manhattan](/wiki/Uptown_Manhattan "Uptown Manhattan") is home to the largest [Dominican immigrant](/wiki/Dominican_Americans#New_York_City "Dominican Americans") community in the United States.[[164]](#cite_note-168) [Harlem](/wiki/Harlem "Harlem"), also in Upper Manhattan, is the historical epicenter of African American culture.[[165]](#cite_note-169) Since 2010, a *[Little Australia](/wiki/Little_Australia "Little Australia")* has emerged and is growing in Nolita, Lower Manhattan.[[166]](#cite_note-LittleAustraliaNYC-170)

Manhattan has two central business districts, the [Financial District](/wiki/Financial_District,_Manhattan "Financial District, Manhattan") at the southern tip of the island, and [Midtown Manhattan](/wiki/Midtown_Manhattan "Midtown Manhattan"). The term *uptown* also refers to the northern part of Manhattan above [72nd Street](/wiki/72nd_Street_(Manhattan) "72nd Street (Manhattan)") and *downtown* to the southern portion below [14th Street](/wiki/14th_Street_(Manhattan) "14th Street (Manhattan)"),[[167]](#cite_note-NYCBasics-171) with *Midtown* covering the area in between, though definitions can be fluid. [Fifth Avenue](/wiki/Fifth_Avenue "Fifth Avenue") roughly bisects Manhattan Island and acts as the demarcation line for east/west designations.[[167]](#cite_note-NYCBasics-171)[[168]](#cite_note-172) South of [Waverly Place](/wiki/Waverly_Place "Waverly Place"), Fifth Avenue terminates and Broadway becomes the east/west demarcation line.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In Manhattan, *uptown* means north and *downtown* means south.[[169]](#cite_note-ManhattanUptownDowntown-173) This usage differs from that of most American cities, where *downtown* refers to the central business district.

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Manhattan&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Demographics")]

Main article: [Demographics of Manhattan](/wiki/Demographics_of_Manhattan "Demographics of Manhattan")

[![Looking at crowds down Broadway](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Broadway_Crowds_%285896264776%29_crop.jpg/250px-Broadway_Crowds_%285896264776%29_crop.jpg)](/wiki/File:Broadway_Crowds_(5896264776)_crop.jpg)

[Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_(Manhattan) "Broadway (Manhattan)") in [Midtown Manhattan](/wiki/Midtown_Manhattan "Midtown Manhattan"). As of the [2020 U.S. census](/wiki/2020_U.S._census "2020 U.S. census"), Manhattan was the most densely populated municipality in the United States.

As of the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), Manhattan's population had increased by 6.8% over the decade to 1,694,251, representing 19.2% of New York City's population of 8,804,190 and 8.4% of New York State's population of 20,201,249.[[5]](#cite_note-ManhattanQuickFacts-5) The population density of New York County was 70,450.8 inhabitants per square mile (27,201.2/km2) in 2022, the highest population density of any county in the United States and [higher than the density of any individual U.S. city](/wiki/List_of_United_States_cities_by_population_density#New_York_City_boroughs "List of United States cities by population density").[[170]](#cite_note-174)[[171]](#cite_note-175) At the [2010 census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census"), there were 1,585,873 people living in Manhattan, an increase of 3.2% from the 1,537,195 counted in the [2000 census](/wiki/2000_United_States_census "2000 United States census").[[172]](#cite_note-176)

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Manhattan&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Religion")]

In 2010, the largest organized religious group in Manhattan was the [Archdiocese of New York](/wiki/Archdiocese_of_New_York "Archdiocese of New York"), with 323,325 Catholics worshiping at 109 parishes, followed by 64,000 [Orthodox](/wiki/Orthodox_Judaism "Orthodox Judaism") Jews with 77 congregations, an estimated 42,545 [Muslims](/wiki/Islam_in_the_United_States "Islam in the United States") with 21 congregations, 42,502 [non-denominational](/wiki/Nondenominational_Christianity "Nondenominational Christianity") adherents with 54 congregations, 26,178 [TEC](/wiki/Episcopal_Church_(United_States) "Episcopal Church (United States)") Episcopalians with 46 congregations, 25,048 [ABC-USA](/wiki/American_Baptist_Churches_USA "American Baptist Churches USA") Baptists with 41 congregations, 24,536 [Reform](/wiki/Reform_Judaism "Reform Judaism") Jews with 10 congregations, 23,982 [Mahayana](/wiki/Mahayana "Mahayana") Buddhists with 35 congregations, 10,503 [PC-USA](/wiki/PC-USA "PC-USA") Presbyterians with 30 congregations, and 10,268 [RCA](/wiki/Reformed_Church_in_America "Reformed Church in America") Presbyterians with 10 congregations. Altogether, 44.0% of the population was claimed as members by religious congregations, although members of historically African-American denominations were underrepresented due to incomplete information.[[177]](#cite_note-181) In 2014, Manhattan had 703 religious organizations, the seventeenth most out of all US counties.[[178]](#cite_note-182) There is a large [Buddhist temple](/wiki/Buddhist_temple "Buddhist temple") in Manhattan located at the foot of the [Manhattan Bridge](/wiki/Manhattan_Bridge "Manhattan Bridge") in [Chinatown](/wiki/Chinatown,_Manhattan "Chinatown, Manhattan").[[179]](#cite_note-183)

### Languages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Manhattan&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Languages")]

As of 2015, 60.0% (927,650) of Manhattan residents, aged five and older, spoke only English at home, while 22.63% (350,112) spoke Spanish, 5.37% (83,013) Chinese, 2.21% (34,246) French, 0.85% (13,138) Korean, 0.72% (11,135) Russian, and 0.70% (10,766) Japanese. In total, 40.0% of Manhattan's population, aged five and older, spoke a language other than English at home.[[180]](#cite_note-184)

## Landmarks and architecture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Manhattan&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Landmarks and architecture")]

Main article: [Architecture of New York City](/wiki/Architecture_of_New_York_City "Architecture of New York City")

See also: [List of skyscrapers in New York City](/wiki/List_of_skyscrapers_in_New_York_City "List of skyscrapers in New York City")

Points of interest on Manhattan Island include the [American Museum of Natural History](/wiki/American_Museum_of_Natural_History "American Museum of Natural History"); the [Battery](/wiki/The_Battery_(Manhattan) "The Battery (Manhattan)"); [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_(Manhattan) "Broadway (Manhattan)") and the [Theater District](/wiki/Theater_District,_Manhattan "Theater District, Manhattan"); [Bryant Park](/wiki/Bryant_Park "Bryant Park"); [Central Park](/wiki/Central_Park "Central Park"), [Chinatown](/wiki/Chinatown,_Manhattan "Chinatown, Manhattan"); the [Chrysler Building](/wiki/Chrysler_Building "Chrysler Building"); [The Cloisters](/wiki/The_Cloisters "The Cloisters"); [Columbia University](/wiki/Columbia_University "Columbia University"); [Curry Hill](/wiki/Curry_Hill "Curry Hill"); the [Empire State Building](/wiki/Empire_State_Building "Empire State Building"); [Flatiron Building](/wiki/Flatiron_Building "Flatiron Building"); the [Financial District](/wiki/Financial_District,_Manhattan "Financial District, Manhattan") (including the [New York Stock Exchange Building](/wiki/New_York_Stock_Exchange_Building "New York Stock Exchange Building"); [Wall Street](/wiki/Wall_Street "Wall Street"); and the [South Street Seaport](/wiki/South_Street_Seaport "South Street Seaport")); [Grand Central Terminal](/wiki/Grand_Central_Terminal "Grand Central Terminal"); [Greenwich Village](/wiki/Greenwich_Village "Greenwich Village") (including [New York University](/wiki/New_York_University "New York University"); [Washington Square Arch](/wiki/Washington_Square_Arch "Washington Square Arch"); and [Stonewall Inn](/wiki/Stonewall_Inn "Stonewall Inn")); [Harlem](/wiki/Harlem "Harlem") and [Spanish Harlem](/wiki/Spanish_Harlem "Spanish Harlem"); the [High Line](/wiki/High_Line "High Line"); [Koreatown](/wiki/Koreatown,_Manhattan "Koreatown, Manhattan"); [Lincoln Center](/wiki/Lincoln_Center "Lincoln Center"); [Little Australia](/wiki/Little_Australia,_Manhattan "Little Australia, Manhattan"); [Little Italy](/wiki/Little_Italy,_Manhattan "Little Italy, Manhattan"); [Madison Square Garden](/wiki/Madison_Square_Garden "Madison Square Garden"); [Museum Mile on Fifth Avenue](/wiki/Fifth_Avenue#Museum_Mile "Fifth Avenue") (including the [Metropolitan Museum of Art](/wiki/Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art "Metropolitan Museum of Art")); [New York Penn Station](/wiki/New_York_Penn_Station "New York Penn Station"), [Port Authority Bus Terminal](/wiki/Port_Authority_Bus_Terminal "Port Authority Bus Terminal"); [Rockefeller Center](/wiki/Rockefeller_Center "Rockefeller Center") (including [Radio City Music Hall](/wiki/Radio_City_Music_Hall "Radio City Music Hall")); [Times Square](/wiki/Times_Square "Times Square"); and the [World Trade Center](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_(2001%E2%80%93present) "World Trade Center (2001–present)") (including the [National September 11 Museum](/wiki/National_September_11_Museum "National September 11 Museum") and [One World Trade Center](/wiki/One_World_Trade_Center "One World Trade Center")).

There are also numerous iconic [bridges](/wiki/Bridges_and_tunnels_in_New_York_City "Bridges and tunnels in New York City") across rivers that connect to Manhattan Island, as well as an emerging number of [supertall skyscrapers](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_New_York_City "List of tallest buildings in New York City"). The [Statue of Liberty](/wiki/Statue_of_Liberty "Statue of Liberty") rests on [Liberty Island](/wiki/Liberty_Island "Liberty Island"), an [exclave](/wiki/Exclave "Exclave") of Manhattan, and part of [Ellis Island](/wiki/Ellis_Island "Ellis Island") is also an exclave of Manhattan. The borough has many [energy-efficient](/wiki/Efficient_energy_use "Efficient energy use") office buildings, such as the [Hearst Tower](/wiki/Hearst_Tower_(New_York_City) "Hearst Tower (New York City)"), the rebuilt [7 World Trade Center](/wiki/7_World_Trade_Center "7 World Trade Center"),[[181]](#cite_note-greenbuilding-185) and the [Bank of America Tower](/wiki/Bank_of_America_Tower_(Manhattan) "Bank of America Tower (Manhattan)")—the first skyscraper designed to attain a Platinum [LEED](/wiki/LEED "LEED") Certification.[[182]](#cite_note-186)[[183]](#cite_note-187)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Park_and_57th_street_Manhattan_New_York_photo_D_Ramey_Logan.jpg/250px-Park_and_57th_street_Manhattan_New_York_photo_D_Ramey_Logan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Park_and_57th_street_Manhattan_New_York_photo_D_Ramey_Logan.jpg)

Many tall buildings have [setbacks](/wiki/Setback_(architecture) "Setback (architecture)") on their facade due to the [1916 Zoning Resolution](/wiki/1916_Zoning_Resolution "1916 Zoning Resolution"), exemplified at [Park Avenue](/wiki/Park_Avenue "Park Avenue") and [57th Street](/wiki/57th_Street_(Manhattan) "57th Street (Manhattan)") in [Midtown Manhattan](/wiki/Midtown_Manhattan "Midtown Manhattan").

The skyscraper, which has shaped Manhattan's distinctive [skyline](/wiki/Skyline "Skyline"), has been closely associated with New York City's identity since the end of the 19th century.[[184]](#cite_note-188) Structures such as the [Equitable Building](/wiki/Equitable_Building_(Manhattan) "Equitable Building (Manhattan)") of 1915, which rises vertically forty stories from the sidewalk, prompted the passage of the [1916 Zoning Resolution](/wiki/1916_Zoning_Resolution "1916 Zoning Resolution"), requiring new buildings to contain [setbacks](/wiki/Setback_(architecture) "Setback (architecture)") withdrawing progressively at a defined angle from the street as they rose, to preserve a view of the sky at street level.[[185]](#cite_note-nyt20160726-189) Manhattan's skyline includes several buildings that are symbolic of New York, in particular the [Chrysler Building](/wiki/Chrysler_Building "Chrysler Building")[[186]](#cite_note-Willis_Friedman-190): 14 and the [Empire State Building](/wiki/Empire_State_Building "Empire State Building"), which sees about 4 million visitors a year.[[187]](#cite_note-nyt20111225-191)

In 1961, the struggling [Pennsylvania Railroad](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Railroad "Pennsylvania Railroad") unveiled plans to tear down the old [Penn Station](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Station_(1910%E2%80%931963) "Pennsylvania Station (1910–1963)") and replace it with a new [Madison Square Garden](/wiki/Madison_Square_Garden "Madison Square Garden") and [office building](/wiki/One_Penn_Plaza "One Penn Plaza") complex.[[188]](#cite_note-192) Organized protests were aimed at preserving the [McKim, Mead & White](/wiki/McKim,_Mead_%26_White "McKim, Mead & White")-designed structure completed in 1910, widely considered a masterpiece of the [Beaux-Arts](/wiki/Beaux-Arts_architecture "Beaux-Arts architecture") style and one of the architectural jewels of New York City.[[189]](#cite_note-193) Despite these efforts, demolition of the structure began in October 1963.[[190]](#cite_note-194) The loss of Penn Station led directly to the enactment in 1965 of a local law establishing the [New York City Landmarks Preservation Commission](/wiki/New_York_City_Landmarks_Preservation_Commission "New York City Landmarks Preservation Commission"), which is responsible for preserving the "city's historic, aesthetic, and [cultural heritage](/wiki/Cultural_heritage "Cultural heritage")".[[191]](#cite_note-195) The [historic preservation](/wiki/Historic_preservation "Historic preservation") movement triggered by Penn Station's demise has been credited with the retention of some one million structures nationwide, including over 1,000 in New York City.[[192]](#cite_note-196) In 2017, a multibillion-dollar rebuilding plan was unveiled to restore the historic grandeur of Penn Station, in the process of upgrading the landmark's status as a critical [transportation hub](/wiki/Transportation_hub "Transportation hub").[[193]](#cite_note-197)

The 700,000 ft2 (65,000 m2) [Moynihan Train Hall](/wiki/Moynihan_Train_Hall "Moynihan Train Hall"), developed as a $1.6 billion renovation and expansion of Penn Station into the [James A. Farley Building](/wiki/James_A._Farley_Building "James A. Farley Building"), the city's former main post office building, was opened in January 2021.[[194]](#cite_note-198)

### Parkland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Manhattan&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Parkland")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Central_Park_-_The_Pond_%2848377220157%29.jpg/250px-Central_Park_-_The_Pond_%2848377220157%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Central_Park_-_The_Pond_(48377220157).jpg)

[Central Park](/wiki/Central_Park "Central Park")

Parkland covers a total of 2,659 acres (10.76 km2), accounting for 18.2% of the borough's land area; the 840-acre (3.4 km2) [Central Park](/wiki/Central_Park "Central Park") is the borough's largest park, comprising 31.6% of Manhattan's parkland.[[195]](#cite_note-ParkList-199) Designed by [Frederick Law Olmsted](/wiki/Frederick_Law_Olmsted "Frederick Law Olmsted") and [Calvert Vaux](/wiki/Calvert_Vaux "Calvert Vaux"), the park is anchored by the 12-acre (4.9 ha) [Great Lawn](/wiki/Great_Lawn "Great Lawn")[[196]](#cite_note-200) and offers extensive [walking tracks](/wiki/Walking_track "Walking track"), two [ice-skating](/wiki/Ice-skating "Ice-skating") rinks, a wildlife sanctuary, and several lawns and sporting areas, as well as 21 playgrounds,[[197]](#cite_note-201) and a 6-mile (9.7 km) road from which automobile traffic has been banned since 2018.[[198]](#cite_note-202) While much of the park looks natural, it is almost entirely landscaped; the [construction of Central Park](/wiki/Construction_of_Central_Park "Construction of Central Park") in the 1850s was one of the era's most massive public works projects, with some 20,000 workers moving 5 million cubic yards (3.8 million cubic meters) of material to shape the [topography](/wiki/Topography "Topography") and create the English-style pastoral landscape that Olmsted and Vaux sought.[[199]](#cite_note-203)

The remaining 70% of Manhattan's parkland includes 204 playgrounds, 251 Greenstreets, 371 [basketball courts](/wiki/Basketball_court "Basketball court"), and many other amenities.[[200]](#cite_note-MBPOEnvironment-204) The next-largest park in Manhattan, the [Hudson River Park](/wiki/Hudson_River_Park "Hudson River Park"), stretches 4.5 miles (7.2 km) along the Hudson River and comprises 550 acres (220 ha).[[201]](#cite_note-RIoG_ParkStats-205) Other major parks include:[[195]](#cite_note-ParkList-199)
