#### Public transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Manhattan&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Public transportation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Grand_Central_Station_Main_Concourse_Jan_2006.jpg/250px-Grand_Central_Station_Main_Concourse_Jan_2006.jpg)](/wiki/File:Grand_Central_Station_Main_Concourse_Jan_2006.jpg)

[Grand Central Terminal](/wiki/Grand_Central_Terminal "Grand Central Terminal"), a [National Historic Landmark](/wiki/National_Historic_Landmark "National Historic Landmark")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/NYC_Downtown_Manhattan_Skyline_seen_from_Paulus_Hook_2019-12-20_IMG_7347_FRD.jpg/250px-NYC_Downtown_Manhattan_Skyline_seen_from_Paulus_Hook_2019-12-20_IMG_7347_FRD.jpg)](/wiki/File:NYC_Downtown_Manhattan_Skyline_seen_from_Paulus_Hook_2019-12-20_IMG_7347_FRD.jpg)

Ferries departing [Battery Park City Ferry Terminal](/wiki/Battery_Park_City_Ferry_Terminal "Battery Park City Ferry Terminal") and helicopters flying above Manhattan

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Staten_Island_Ferry-Battery_Park-2012.jpg/250px-Staten_Island_Ferry-Battery_Park-2012.jpg)](/wiki/File:Staten_Island_Ferry-Battery_Park-2012.jpg)

The [Staten Island Ferry](/wiki/Staten_Island_Ferry "Staten Island Ferry"), seen from [the Battery](/wiki/The_Battery_(Manhattan) "The Battery (Manhattan)"), crosses [Upper New York Bay](/wiki/Upper_New_York_Bay "Upper New York Bay"), providing free public transportation between [Staten Island](/wiki/Staten_Island "Staten Island") and Manhattan.

Manhattan is unique in the U.S. for intense use of public transportation and lack of private car ownership. While 88% of Americans nationwide drive to their jobs, with only 5% using public transport, [mass transit](/wiki/Mass_transit "Mass transit") is the dominant form of travel for residents of Manhattan, with 72% of borough residents using public transport to get to work, while only 18% drove.[[353]](#cite_note-358)[[354]](#cite_note-359) According to the 2000 United States Census, 77.5% of Manhattan households do not own a car.[[355]](#cite_note-360) Congestion pricing was [implemented in Lower and Midtown Manhattan](/wiki/Congestion_pricing_in_New_York_City "Congestion pricing in New York City") in January 2025 and applies to nearly all motor vehicular traffic in Manhattan south of [60th Street](/wiki/60th_Street_(Manhattan) "60th Street (Manhattan)").[[356]](#cite_note-ManhattanCongestionPricing-361)

The [New York City Subway](/wiki/New_York_City_Subway "New York City Subway") is the primary means of travel within the city, linking every borough except Staten Island. There are [151 subway stations in Manhattan](/wiki/List_of_New_York_City_Subway_stations_in_Manhattan "List of New York City Subway stations in Manhattan"), out of the 472 stations.[[357]](#cite_note-362) A second subway, the [PATH](/wiki/PATH_(rail_system) "PATH (rail system)") system, connects [six stations in Manhattan](/wiki/List_of_PATH_stations "List of PATH stations") to northern [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey").[[358]](#cite_note-363) [Commuter rail](/wiki/Regional_rail "Regional rail") services operating to and from Manhattan are the [Long Island Rail Road](/wiki/Long_Island_Rail_Road "Long Island Rail Road") (LIRR), which connects Manhattan and other New York City boroughs to [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island"); the [Metro-North Railroad](/wiki/Metro-North_Railroad "Metro-North Railroad"), which connects Manhattan to Upstate New York and Southwestern Connecticut; and [NJ Transit](/wiki/NJ_Transit "NJ Transit") trains, which run to various points in New Jersey. The commuter rail lines converge at [New York Penn Station](/wiki/New_York_Penn_Station "New York Penn Station") and [Grand Central Terminal](/wiki/Grand_Central_Terminal "Grand Central Terminal"), on the west and east sides of Midtown Manhattan, respectively. They are the two busiest rail stations in the United States.[[359]](#cite_note-364) [Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak") provides inter-city passenger rail service from Penn Station to [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston"), [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia"), [Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore "Baltimore"), and Washington, D.C.; [Upstate New York](/wiki/Upstate_New_York "Upstate New York") and [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England"); cross-Canadian border service to [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto") and [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal"); and destinations in the [Southern](/wiki/Southern_United_States "Southern United States") and [Midwestern United States](/wiki/Midwestern_United_States "Midwestern United States").

The [East Side Access](/wiki/East_Side_Access "East Side Access") project, which brings LIRR trains to [Grand Central Terminal](/wiki/Grand_Central_Terminal "Grand Central Terminal"), opened in 2023.[[360]](#cite_note-365)[[361]](#cite_note-366) Four multi-billion-dollar projects were completed in the mid-2010s: the $1.4 billion [Fulton Center](/wiki/Fulton_Center "Fulton Center") in November 2014,[[362]](#cite_note-367) the $2.4 billion [7 Subway Extension](/wiki/7_Subway_Extension "7 Subway Extension") in September 2015,[[363]](#cite_note-368) the $4 billion [World Trade Center Transportation Hub](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_Transportation_Hub "World Trade Center Transportation Hub") in March 2016,[[364]](#cite_note-369)[[365]](#cite_note-370) and Phase 1 of the $4.5 billion [Second Avenue Subway](/wiki/Second_Avenue_Subway "Second Avenue Subway") in January 2017.[[366]](#cite_note-371)[[367]](#cite_note-372)

[MTA New York City Transit](/wiki/MTA_New_York_City_Transit "MTA New York City Transit") operates local buses within Manhattan under the brand [New York City Bus](/wiki/New_York_City_Bus "New York City Bus"). An extensive network of express bus routes serves commuters and other travelers heading into Manhattan.[[368]](#cite_note-373)

The [Roosevelt Island Tramway](/wiki/Roosevelt_Island_Tramway "Roosevelt Island Tramway"), one of two commuter [cable car](/wiki/Aerial_tramway "Aerial tramway") systems in North America, takes commuters between [Roosevelt Island](/wiki/Roosevelt_Island "Roosevelt Island") and Manhattan Island.[[369]](#cite_note-374)[[370]](#cite_note-375) The [Staten Island Ferry](/wiki/Staten_Island_Ferry "Staten Island Ferry") carries over 21 million passengers annually on the 5.2-mile (8.4 km) run between Manhattan and Staten Island.[[371]](#cite_note-376)[[372]](#cite_note-377) The ferry has been fare-free since 1997.[[373]](#cite_note-378) Manhattan is also served by [NYC Ferry](/wiki/NYC_Ferry "NYC Ferry"), which began operating in 2017.[[374]](#cite_note-379)[[375]](#cite_note-:14-380) All of the system's routes serve Manhattan.[[376]](#cite_note-381)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/Port-authority-terminal.jpg/250px-Port-authority-terminal.jpg)](/wiki/File:Port-authority-terminal.jpg)

The [Port Authority Bus Terminal](/wiki/Port_Authority_Bus_Terminal "Port Authority Bus Terminal"), at [Eighth Avenue](/wiki/Eighth_Avenue_(Manhattan) "Eighth Avenue (Manhattan)") and [42nd Street](/wiki/42nd_Street_(Manhattan) "42nd Street (Manhattan)"), is the world's busiest bus station.[[377]](#cite_note-Record2021-382)[[378]](#cite_note-PABT2008-383)

The [Port Authority Bus Terminal](/wiki/Port_Authority_Bus_Terminal "Port Authority Bus Terminal") is the city's main [intercity bus](/wiki/Intercity_bus "Intercity bus") terminal and the world's busiest bus station. It serves 250,000 passengers on 7,000 buses each workday in a 1950 building designed to accommodate 60,000 daily passengers. A 2021 plan announced by the Port Authority would spend $10 billion to expand capacity and modernize the facility.[[378]](#cite_note-PABT2008-383)[[379]](#cite_note-NYT2021-384)[[377]](#cite_note-Record2021-382) In 2024, the Port Authority announced plans for a new terminal that would be completed by 2032 and include a pair of office buildings to defray the costs of the project.[[380]](#cite_note-385)

#### Taxis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Manhattan&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Taxis")]

Main article: [Taxis of New York City](/wiki/Taxis_of_New_York_City "Taxis of New York City")

New York's iconic yellow [taxicabs](/wiki/Taxicab "Taxicab"), which number 13,087 citywide and must have a medallion authorizing the pickup of street hails, are ubiquitous in the borough.[[381]](#cite_note-386) Private [vehicle for hire](/wiki/Vehicle_for_hire "Vehicle for hire") companies provide significant competition for taxicabs.[[382]](#cite_note-387)

#### Bicycles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Manhattan&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Bicycles")]

Main article: [Cycling in New York City](/wiki/Cycling_in_New_York_City "Cycling in New York City")

According to the [government of New York City](/wiki/Government_of_New_York_City "Government of New York City"), Manhattan had 19,676 [bicycle commuters](/wiki/Cycling_in_New_York_City "Cycling in New York City") in 2017, roughly doubling from its total of 9,613 in 2012.[[383]](#cite_note-388)

#### Streets and roads

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Manhattan&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Streets and roads")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/Manhattanhenge_2016-07-12-FRD.png/250px-Manhattanhenge_2016-07-12-FRD.png)](/wiki/File:Manhattanhenge_2016-07-12-FRD.png)

Tourists observing [Manhattanhenge](/wiki/Manhattanhenge "Manhattanhenge") on [42nd Street](/wiki/42nd_Street_(Manhattan) "42nd Street (Manhattan)") on July 12, 2016

The [Commissioners' Plan of 1811](/wiki/Commissioners%27_Plan_of_1811 "Commissioners' Plan of 1811") called for twelve numbered "avenues" running north and south roughly parallel to the [Hudson River](/wiki/Hudson_River "Hudson River"), each 100 feet (30 m) wide, with [First Avenue](/wiki/First_Avenue_(Manhattan) "First Avenue (Manhattan)") on the east side and [Twelfth Avenue](/wiki/Twelfth_Avenue "Twelfth Avenue") on the west side.[[60]](#cite_note-MCNY1811-62)[[384]](#cite_note-389) There are several intermittent avenues east of First Avenue, including four additional lettered avenues running from [Avenue A](/wiki/Avenue_A_(Manhattan) "Avenue A (Manhattan)") eastward to [Avenue D](/wiki/Avenue_D_(Manhattan) "Avenue D (Manhattan)") in an area now known as [Alphabet City](/wiki/Alphabet_City "Alphabet City").[[385]](#cite_note-390) The numbered streets in Manhattan run east–west, and are generally 60 feet (18 m) wide, with about 200 feet (61 m) between streets.[[60]](#cite_note-MCNY1811-62) The [address algorithm of Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan_address_algorithm "Manhattan address algorithm") is used to estimate the closest east–west cross street for building numbers on north–south avenues.[[386]](#cite_note-391)

According to the original Commissioner's Plan, there were [155](/wiki/155th_Street_(Manhattan) "155th Street (Manhattan)") numbered crosstown streets,[[387]](#cite_note-392) but later the grid was extended up to the northernmost corner of Manhattan Island, where the last numbered street is [220th Street](/wiki/220th_Street_(Manhattan) "220th Street (Manhattan)"), though the grid continues to 228th Street in the borough's [Marble Hill](/wiki/Marble_Hill,_Manhattan "Marble Hill, Manhattan") neighborhood.[[388]](#cite_note-393)[[389]](#cite_note-NYT2002-394) Fifteen crosstown streets were designated as 100 feet (30 m) wide, including [34th](/wiki/34th_Street_(Manhattan) "34th Street (Manhattan)"), [42nd](/wiki/42nd_Street_(Manhattan) "42nd Street (Manhattan)"), [57th](/wiki/57th_Street_(Manhattan) "57th Street (Manhattan)") and [125th](/wiki/125th_Street_(Manhattan) "125th Street (Manhattan)") Streets,[[390]](#cite_note-395) which became some of the borough's most significant transportation and [shopping](/wiki/Shopping "Shopping") venues. [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_(Manhattan) "Broadway (Manhattan)"), following the route of a Native American trail, is the most notable of many exceptions to the grid, starting at [Bowling Green](/wiki/Bowling_Green_(New_York_City) "Bowling Green (New York City)") in Lower Manhattan and continuing north for 13 miles (21 km) into the Bronx.[[391]](#cite_note-396) In much of Midtown Manhattan, Broadway runs at a diagonal to the grid, creating major named intersections at [Union Square](/wiki/Union_Square_(New_York_City) "Union Square (New York City)"), [Madison Square](/wiki/Madison_Square "Madison Square"), [Herald Square](/wiki/Herald_Square "Herald Square"), [Times Square](/wiki/Times_Square "Times Square"), and [Columbus Circle](/wiki/Columbus_Circle "Columbus Circle").[[392]](#cite_note-397)[[393]](#cite_note-398)

"Crosstown streets" refers primarily to major east-west streets connecting Manhattan's [East Side](/wiki/East_Side_(Manhattan) "East Side (Manhattan)") and [West Side](/wiki/West_Side_(Manhattan) "West Side (Manhattan)"). The trip is notoriously frustrating for drivers because of heavy [congestion](/wiki/Traffic_congestion "Traffic congestion") on narrow local streets; absence of express roads other than the [Trans-Manhattan Expressway](/wiki/Trans-Manhattan_Expressway "Trans-Manhattan Expressway") at the far north end of Manhattan Island; and restricted to very limited crosstown automobile travel within [Central Park](/wiki/Central_Park "Central Park"). Proposals to build highways traversing the island through Manhattan's densest neighborhoods, namely the [Mid-Manhattan Expressway](/wiki/Mid-Manhattan_Expressway "Mid-Manhattan Expressway") across [34th Street](/wiki/34th_Street_(Manhattan) "34th Street (Manhattan)") and the [Lower Manhattan Expressway](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan_Expressway "Lower Manhattan Expressway") through [SoHo](/wiki/SoHo "SoHo"), failed in the 1960s.[[394]](#cite_note-399)[[395]](#cite_note-400) In New York City, all turns at red lights are illegal unless a sign permitting such maneuvers is present, significantly shaping traffic patterns in Manhattan.[[396]](#cite_note-401)

Another consequence of the strict grid plan of most of Manhattan, and the grid's skew of approximately 28.9 degrees, is a phenomenon sometimes referred to as [Manhattanhenge](/wiki/Manhattanhenge "Manhattanhenge") (by analogy with [Stonehenge](/wiki/Stonehenge "Stonehenge")).[[397]](#cite_note-Manhattanhenge-402) On May 28 and July 12, the sunset is aligned with the street grid lines, with the result that the sun is visible at or near the western horizon from street level.[[397]](#cite_note-Manhattanhenge-402)[[398]](#cite_note-403) A similar phenomenon occurs with the sunrise on the eastern horizon on December 5 and January 8.[[399]](#cite_note-404)

The [FDR Drive](/wiki/FDR_Drive "FDR Drive") and [Harlem River Drive](/wiki/Harlem_River_Drive "Harlem River Drive"), both designed by controversial New York master planner [Robert Moses](/wiki/Robert_Moses "Robert Moses"),[[400]](#cite_note-405) comprise a single, long [limited-access](/wiki/Limited-access "Limited-access") [parkway](/wiki/Parkway_(New_York) "Parkway (New York)") skirting the east side of Manhattan along the [East River](/wiki/East_River "East River") and [Harlem River](/wiki/Harlem_River "Harlem River") south of [Dyckman Street](/wiki/Dyckman_Street "Dyckman Street"). The [Henry Hudson Parkway](/wiki/Henry_Hudson_Parkway "Henry Hudson Parkway") is the corresponding parkway on the West Side north of [57th Street](/wiki/57th_Street_(Manhattan) "57th Street (Manhattan)").

See also: [List of numbered streets in Manhattan](/wiki/List_of_numbered_streets_in_Manhattan "List of numbered streets in Manhattan") and [List of eponymous streets in New York City](/wiki/List_of_eponymous_streets_in_New_York_City "List of eponymous streets in New York City")

#### Bridges, tunnels, and ferries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Manhattan&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Bridges, tunnels, and ferries")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/2016_One_World_Observatory_view_southsoutheast_towards_Brooklyn_and_Manhattan_Bridges.jpg/250px-2016_One_World_Observatory_view_southsoutheast_towards_Brooklyn_and_Manhattan_Bridges.jpg)](/wiki/File:2016_One_World_Observatory_view_southsoutheast_towards_Brooklyn_and_Manhattan_Bridges.jpg)

The [Brooklyn Bridge](/wiki/Brooklyn_Bridge "Brooklyn Bridge") (on right) and [Manhattan Bridge](/wiki/Manhattan_Bridge "Manhattan Bridge") (on left), two of three bridges that connect [Lower Manhattan](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan "Lower Manhattan") with [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn") over the [East River](/wiki/East_River "East River").

Being primarily an island, Manhattan is linked to New York City's outer boroughs by bridges. Manhattan has fixed highway connections with [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey") to its west by way of the [George Washington Bridge](/wiki/George_Washington_Bridge "George Washington Bridge"), the [Holland Tunnel](/wiki/Holland_Tunnel "Holland Tunnel"), and the [Lincoln Tunnel](/wiki/Lincoln_Tunnel "Lincoln Tunnel"), and to three of the four other New York City boroughs—[the Bronx](/wiki/The_Bronx "The Bronx") to the northeast, and [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn") and [Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens") (both on [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island")) to the east and south. Its only direct connection with the fifth New York City borough, [Staten Island](/wiki/Staten_Island "Staten Island"), is the free [Staten Island Ferry](/wiki/Staten_Island_Ferry "Staten Island Ferry") across [New York Harbor](/wiki/New_York_Harbor "New York Harbor"), located near [Battery Park](/wiki/Battery_Park "Battery Park") at Manhattan's southern tip. It is also possible to travel on land to Staten Island by way of Brooklyn, via the [Verrazzano–Narrows Bridge](/wiki/Verrazzano%E2%80%93Narrows_Bridge "Verrazzano–Narrows Bridge").

The 14-lane George Washington Bridge, the world's busiest motor vehicle bridge,[[401]](#cite_note-panynj.gov-406)[[402]](#cite_note-abcgwb-407) connects [Washington Heights](/wiki/Washington_Heights,_Manhattan "Washington Heights, Manhattan"), in [Upper Manhattan](/wiki/Upper_Manhattan "Upper Manhattan") to [Bergen County](/wiki/Bergen_County "Bergen County") in [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey").[[403]](#cite_note-408) There are numerous bridges to the Bronx across the [Harlem River](/wiki/Harlem_River "Harlem River"), and five (listed north to south)—the [Triborough](/wiki/Triborough_Bridge "Triborough Bridge") (known officially as the Robert F. Kennedy Bridge), [Ed Koch Queensboro](/wiki/Queensboro_Bridge "Queensboro Bridge") (also known as the 59th Street Bridge), [Williamsburg](/wiki/Williamsburg_Bridge "Williamsburg Bridge"), [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan_Bridge "Manhattan Bridge"), and [Brooklyn Bridges](/wiki/Brooklyn_Bridge "Brooklyn Bridge")—that cross the [East River](/wiki/East_River "East River") to connect Manhattan to Long Island.[[404]](#cite_note-409)

Several tunnels also link Manhattan Island to New York City's outer boroughs and New Jersey. The [Lincoln Tunnel](/wiki/Lincoln_Tunnel "Lincoln Tunnel"), which carries 120,000 vehicles a day under the Hudson River between New Jersey and [Midtown Manhattan](/wiki/Midtown_Manhattan "Midtown Manhattan"), is the busiest vehicular tunnel in the world.[[405]](#cite_note-410) The tunnel was built instead of a bridge to allow unfettered passage of large passenger and [cargo ships](/wiki/Cargo_ship "Cargo ship") that sail through New York Harbor and up the Hudson River to Manhattan's piers. The [Holland Tunnel](/wiki/Holland_Tunnel "Holland Tunnel"), connecting Lower Manhattan to [Jersey City, New Jersey](/wiki/Jersey_City,_New_Jersey "Jersey City, New Jersey"), was the world's first mechanically ventilated vehicular tunnel.[[406]](#cite_note-411) The [Queens–Midtown Tunnel](/wiki/Queens%E2%80%93Midtown_Tunnel "Queens–Midtown Tunnel"), built to relieve congestion on the bridges connecting Manhattan with Queens and Brooklyn, was the largest non-federal project in its time when it was completed in 1940;[[407]](#cite_note-412) President [Franklin D. Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Franklin D. Roosevelt") was the first person to drive through it.[[408]](#cite_note-413) The [Brooklyn–Battery Tunnel](/wiki/Brooklyn%E2%80%93Battery_Tunnel "Brooklyn–Battery Tunnel") runs underneath [Battery Park](/wiki/Battery_Park "Battery Park") and connects the [Financial District](/wiki/Financial_District,_Manhattan "Financial District, Manhattan") at the southern tip of Manhattan to [Red Hook](/wiki/Red_Hook,_Brooklyn "Red Hook, Brooklyn") in Brooklyn.

Several ferry services operate between New Jersey and Manhattan.[[409]](#cite_note-414) These ferries mainly serve midtown, Battery Park City, and Wall Street.

#### Heliports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Manhattan&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Heliports")]

Manhattan has three public heliports: the [East 34th Street Heliport](/wiki/East_34th_Street_Heliport "East 34th Street Heliport") (also known as the Atlantic Metro-port), owned by New York City and run by the [New York City Economic Development Corporation](/wiki/New_York_City_Economic_Development_Corporation "New York City Economic Development Corporation") (NYCEDC); the [Port Authority Downtown Manhattan/Wall Street Heliport](/wiki/Downtown_Manhattan_Heliport "Downtown Manhattan Heliport"), owned by the [Port Authority of New York and New Jersey](/wiki/Port_Authority_of_New_York_and_New_Jersey "Port Authority of New York and New Jersey") and run by the NYCEDC; and the [West 30th Street Heliport](/wiki/West_30th_Street_Heliport "West 30th Street Heliport"), owned by the Hudson River Park Trust.[[410]](#cite_note-415)

### Utilities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Manhattan&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Utilities")]

Gas and electric service is provided by [Consolidated Edison](/wiki/Consolidated_Edison "Consolidated Edison"). Manhattan witnessed the doubling of its natural gas supply when a new [gas pipeline](/wiki/Gas_pipeline "Gas pipeline") opened on November 1, 2013.[[411]](#cite_note-416) [Con Edison operates](/wiki/Con_Edison_steam_operations "Con Edison steam operations") the world's largest [district steam](/wiki/District_heating "District heating") system, which consists of 105 miles (169 km) of steam pipes, providing steam for heating, hot water, and air conditioning[[412]](#cite_note-417) by some 1,800 Manhattan customers.[[413]](#cite_note-418) Cable service is provided by [Time Warner Cable](/wiki/Time_Warner_Cable "Time Warner Cable") and telephone service is provided by [Verizon Communications](/wiki/Verizon_Communications "Verizon Communications"), although [AT&T](/wiki/AT%26T "AT&T") is available as well.

The [New York City Department of Sanitation](/wiki/New_York_City_Department_of_Sanitation "New York City Department of Sanitation") is responsible for garbage removal.[[414]](#cite_note-419) The bulk of the city's trash is disposed at mega-dumps in Pennsylvania, Virginia, South Carolina, and Ohio (via transfer stations in New Jersey, Brooklyn and Queens) since the 2001 closure of the [Fresh Kills Landfill](/wiki/Fresh_Kills_Landfill "Fresh Kills Landfill") on [Staten Island](/wiki/Staten_Island "Staten Island").[[415]](#cite_note-420) A small amount of trash processed at transfer sites in New Jersey is sometimes incinerated at [waste-to-energy](/wiki/Waste-to-energy "Waste-to-energy") facilities.

New York City has the largest clean-air diesel-[hybrid](/wiki/Hybrid_vehicle "Hybrid vehicle") and [compressed natural gas](/wiki/Compressed_natural_gas "Compressed natural gas") bus fleet, which also operates in Manhattan, in the country. It also has some of the first hybrid taxis, most of which operate in Manhattan.[[416]](#cite_note-421)

### Health care

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Manhattan&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Health care")]

Main article: [List of hospitals in New York City § Manhattan](/wiki/List_of_hospitals_in_New_York_City#Manhattan "List of hospitals in New York City")

There are many hospitals in Manhattan, including two of the 25 largest in the United States (as of 2017):[[417]](#cite_note-422)

* [Bellevue Hospital](/wiki/Bellevue_Hospital "Bellevue Hospital")
* [Lenox Hill Hospital](/wiki/Lenox_Hill_Hospital "Lenox Hill Hospital")
* [Lower Manhattan Hospital](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan_Hospital "Lower Manhattan Hospital")
* [Metropolitan Hospital Center](/wiki/Metropolitan_Hospital_Center "Metropolitan Hospital Center")
* [Mount Sinai Beth Israel Hospital](/wiki/Mount_Sinai_Beth_Israel "Mount Sinai Beth Israel") (closed, April 2025)[[418]](#cite_note-423)
* [Mount Sinai Hospital](/wiki/Mount_Sinai_Hospital_(Manhattan) "Mount Sinai Hospital (Manhattan)")
* [NewYork–Presbyterian Hospital](/wiki/NewYork%E2%80%93Presbyterian_Hospital "NewYork–Presbyterian Hospital")
* [NYC Health + Hospitals/Harlem](/wiki/NYC_Health_%2B_Hospitals/Harlem "NYC Health + Hospitals/Harlem")
* [NYU Langone Medical Center](/wiki/NYU_Langone_Medical_Center "NYU Langone Medical Center")

### Water purity and availability

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Manhattan&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: Water purity and availability")]

Main articles: [Food and water in New York City](/wiki/Food_and_water_in_New_York_City "Food and water in New York City") and [New York City water supply system](/wiki/New_York_City_water_supply_system "New York City water supply system")

New York City is supplied with drinking water by the protected [Catskill Mountains](/wiki/Catskill_Mountains "Catskill Mountains") watershed.[[419]](#cite_note-424) As a result of the watershed's integrity and undisturbed [natural water filtration system](/wiki/Water_purification "Water purification"), New York is one of only four major cities in the US with a majority of drinking water pure enough not to require [water treatment](/wiki/Water_treatment "Water treatment").[[420]](#cite_note-425) The [Croton Watershed](/wiki/Croton_Falls_Reservoir "Croton Falls Reservoir") north of the city is undergoing construction of a US$3.2 billion water purification plant to augment New York City's water supply by an estimated 290 million gallons daily, representing a greater than 20% addition to the city's water availability.[[421]](#cite_note-426) Water comes to Manhattan through the [tunnels 1 and 2](/wiki/New_York_City_water_supply_system#Tunnels "New York City water supply system"), and in the future through [Tunnel No. 3](/wiki/New_York_City_Water_Tunnel_No._3 "New York City Water Tunnel No. 3"), begun in 1970.[[422]](#cite_note-427)

Retrieved May 15, 2007. "It is at a triangular site where Broadway and Fifth Avenue—the two most important streets of New York—meet at Madison Square, and because of the juxtaposition of the streets and the park across the street, there was a wind-tunnel effect here. In the early twentieth century, men would hang out on the corner here on Twenty-third Street and watch the wind blowing women's dresses up so that they could catch a little bit of ankle. This entered into popular culture and there are hundreds of postcards and illustrations of women with their dresses blowing up in front of the Flatiron Building. And it supposedly is where the slang expression "23 skidoo" comes from because the police would come and give the voyeurs the 23 skidoo to tell them to get out of the area."

### Sources

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Manhattan&action=edit&section=55 "Edit section: Sources")]

* Burke, Katie. ed. *Manhattan Memories: A Book of Postcards of Old New York* (2000); Postcards lacking the (c) symbol are in the public domain.
* Jackson, Kenneth T. and David S. Dunbar, eds. *Empire City: New York Through the Centuries* (2005), 1015 pages of [excerpts](https://www.amazon.com/Empire-City-York-Through-Centuries/dp/0231109091/)
* Still, Bayrd, ed. *Mirror for Gotham: New York as Seen by Contemporaries from Dutch Days to the Present* (New York University Press, 1956)
* Virga, Vincent, ed. *Historic Maps and Views of New York* (2008)
* Stokes, I.N. Phelps. *The Iconography of Manhattan Island, 1498–1909 compiled from original sources and illustrated by photo-intaglio reproductions of important maps plans views and documents in public and private collections*  (6 vols., 1915–28). A highly detailed, heavily illustrated chronology of Manhattan and New York City. see [The Iconography of Manhattan Island](/wiki/The_Iconography_of_Manhattan_Island "The Iconography of Manhattan Island") All volumes are on line free at:
  + [I.N. Phelps Stokes; The Iconography of Manhattan Island Vol 1. 1915](https://archive.org/stream/iconographyofman01stok#page/n13/mode/2up) v. 1. The period of discovery (1524–1609); the Dutch period (1609–1664). The English period (1664–1763). The Revolutionary period (1763–1783). Period of adjustment and reconstruction; New York as the state and federal capital (1783–1811)
  + [I.N. Phelps Stokes; The Iconography of Manhattan Island Vol 2. 1916](https://archive.org/stream/iconographyofma_02stok#page/n13/mode/2up) v. 2. Cartography: an essay on the development of knowledge regarding the geography of the east coast of North America; Manhattan Island and its environs on early maps and charts / by F.C. Wieder and I.N. Phelps Stokes. The Manatus maps. The Castello plan. The Dutch grants. Early New York newspapers (1725–1811). Plan of Manhattan Island in 1908
  + [I.N. Phelps Stokes; The Iconography of Manhattan Island Vol 3. 1918](https://archive.org/stream/iconographyofman_b03stok#page/n11/mode/2up) v. 3. The War of 1812 (1812–1815). Period of invention, prosperity, and progress (1815–1841). Period of industrial and educational development (1842–1860). The Civil War (1861–1865); period of political and social development (1865–1876). The modern city and island (1876–1909)
  + [I.N. Phelps Stokes; The Iconography of Manhattan Island Vol 4. 1922](https://archive.org/stream/iconographyofman_a04stok#page/n13/mode/2up); v. 4. The period of discovery (565–1626); the Dutch period (1626–1664). The English period (1664–1763). The Revolutionary period, part I (1763–1776)
  + [I.N. Phelps Stokes; The Iconography of Manhattan Island Vol 5. 1926](https://archive.org/stream/iconographyofman05stok#page/n13/mode/2up); v. 5. The Revolutionary period, part II (1776–1783). Period of adjustment and reconstruction New York as the state and federal capital (1783–1811). The War of 1812 (1812–1815); period of invention, prosperity, and progress (1815–1841). Period of industrial and educational development (1842–1860). The Civil War (1861–1865); Period of political and social development (1865–1876). The modern city and island (1876–1909)
  + [I.N. Phelps Stokes; The Iconography of Manhattan Island Vol 6. 1928](https://archive.org/stream/iconographyofman06stok#page/n13/mode/2up); v. 6. Chronology: addenda. Original grants and farms. Bibliography. Index.

* [Burns, Ric](/wiki/Ric_Burns "Ric Burns"), and [Sanders, James](/wiki/James_Sanders_(architect) "James Sanders (architect)"). *New York: An Illustrated History* (2003), book version of 17-hour Burns [PBS](/wiki/PBS "PBS") documentary, *[New York: A Documentary Film](/wiki/New_York:_A_Documentary_Film "New York: A Documentary Film")*
* [Burrows, Edwin G.](/wiki/Edwin_G._Burrows "Edwin G. Burrows") and [Wallace, Mike](/wiki/Mike_Wallace_(historian) "Mike Wallace (historian)") (1999). *[Gotham: A History of New York City to 1898](/wiki/Gotham:_A_History_of_New_York_City_to_1898 "Gotham: A History of New York City to 1898")*. New York: [Oxford University Press](/wiki/Oxford_University_Press "Oxford University Press"). [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [0-195-11634-8](/wiki/Special:BookSources/0-195-11634-8 "Special:BookSources/0-195-11634-8")., The standard scholarly history, 1390pp
* Ellis, Edward Robb. *The Epic of New York City: A Narrative History* (2004) 640pp; [Excerpt and text search](https://www.amazon.com/The-Epic-New-York-City/dp/0786714360/); Popular history concentrating on violent events & scandals
* Homberger, Eric. *The Historical Atlas of New York City: A Visual Celebration of 400 Years of New York City's History* (2005)
* [Jackson, Kenneth T.](/wiki/Kenneth_T._Jackson "Kenneth T. Jackson"), ed. (2010). *[The Encyclopedia of New York City](/wiki/The_Encyclopedia_of_New_York_City "The Encyclopedia of New York City")* (2nd ed.). New Haven: [Yale University Press](/wiki/Yale_University_Press "Yale University Press"). [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-0-300-11465-2](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-300-11465-2 "Special:BookSources/978-0-300-11465-2").
* Kouwenhoven, John Atlee. *The Columbia Historical Portrait of New York: An Essay in Graphic History*. (1953)
* Lankevich, George J. *New York City: A Short History* (2002)
* McCully, Betsy. *City at the Water's Edge: A Natural History of New York* (2005), environmental history [excerpt and text search](https://www.amazon.com/CITY-AT-WATERS-EDGE-Natural/dp/B0085XRE08/)
* Reitano, Joanne. *The Restless City: A Short History of New York from Colonial Times to the Present* (2010), Popular history with focus on politics and riots [excerpt and text search](https://www.amazon.com/The-Restless-City-History-Colonial/dp/0415880149/)
* [Filler, Martin](/wiki/Filler,_Martin "Filler, Martin") (April 2015). *[New York: Conspicuous Construction](http://www.nybooks.com/articles/archives/2015/apr/02/new-york-conspicuous-construction/).* Analysis of architectural and social aspects of "ultra-luxury towers ... the smokestack-like protuberances that now disrupt the skyline of midtown Manhattan." *[The New York Review of Books](/wiki/The_New_York_Review_of_Books "The New York Review of Books")*
* Story, Louise and Saul, Stephanie (February 2015). *[Towers of Secrecy](https://www.nytimes.com/2015/02/08/nyregion/stream-of-foreign-wealth-flows-to-time-warner-condos.html).* A series of 6 articles "examining people behind shell companies buying high-end real estate" in midtown Manhattan. Part 1: Time Warner Center: Symbol of the Boom, Part 2: The Mysterious Malaysian Financier, Part 3: The Besieged Indian Builder, Part 4: The Mexican Power Brokers, Part 5: The Russian Minister and Friends, Summary: The Hidden Money Buying Up New York Real Estate. *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")*
